message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Final red-team review">
You are a hiring reviewer at SPAN assessing a candidate's backend coding test submission. You are contrarian and hard to impress. Your job is to find the reasons this should NOT pass — and be right about them.

Repo: /Users/jramirezolmos/Documents/personal/span-league-table (also public at https://github.com/ingjramirez/span-league-table). The brief is "BE Coding Test 2026.pdf" in the repo root — READ IT FIRST, in full. It uses RFC-2119 terms (MUST/SHOULD); treat it as the spec.

This submission has already survived one QA pass and one red-team pass; bugs found then have been fixed. Do not assume the earlier reviews were right or complete — start fresh. Your value is in what everyone else missed.

Read everything: README.md, CLAUDE.md, AI_REFLECTION.md, src/, tests/, data/, pyproject.toml, .claude/, ai/, and `git log`. Run the tests (`.venv/bin/python -m pytest`). Run the CLI. Try to break it.

Assess:
1. **Completeness against the brief.** Every MUST and SHOULD, line by line. Any mandatory artifact missing, empty, or a token gesture? (README, CLAUDE.md, AI_REFLECTION.md, .claude/, ai/ conversation history, input AND output CSVs, automated tests, no committed packages, documented setup.) Does `git clone` + the documented setup steps actually work from scratch? Try it in a temp dir from the GitHub remote.
2. **Correctness of the ANSWER.** The deliverable is the standings for the English First Division in the 10th week of 1974/75. Verify the rules (2 pts a win; goal average NOT goal difference) and the data/table against sources YOURSELF — worldfootball.net and 11v11.com block plain fetches, so use the Chrome browser tools (load via ToolSearch: mcp__claude-in-chrome__*). The repo ships THREE readings of "10th week" (rounds 1-10; played-by-28-Sep-1974; played-by-19-Oct-1974). Is that thorough or is it hedging/indecisive? Would a reviewer respect it or think they couldn't commit?
3. **Code quality.** Bugs, over-engineering, under-testing. The suite claims 100% branch coverage — find code that is executed but not actually asserted on. Try mutating the implementation and see if tests catch it.
4. **The AI collaboration artifacts.** Is AI_REFLECTION.md substantive and credible, or self-serving? Does the git history misrepresent how the work was done? (The first commit explicitly discloses that commits group finished work rather than replaying the build — is that disclosure adequate or is the history still misleading?) Does ai/ actually contain the real session?
5. **Would you hire?** Where is the candidate's own judgement visible vs where does it read as AI output the candidate didn't think about?

Every criticism must be backed by a quote from the brief or a file:line. Do not be contrarian for its own sake. If something is genuinely strong, say so briefly and move on.

Do NOT modify anything in the project directory (read-only; the repo is pushed to GitHub — do not push, do not open PRs, do not modify the remote). Use /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-jramirezolmos-Documents-personal-span-league-table/ed232752-4a2f-47aa-9632-c8175fcab651/scratchpad for any scratch work, including a fresh clone.

Deliver: verdict (PASS / BORDERLINE / FAIL) with reasoning, then a ranked list of objections (most damaging first) each with evidence and the specific change needed, then the single strongest thing about the submission.
</teammate-message>

<system-reminder>
Other agents active in this session, addressable via SendMessage({to: name, message}): main, qa-reviewer, span-red-team.
</system-reminder>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- telegram:access: Manage Telegram channel access — approve pairings, edit allowlists, set DM/group policy. Use when the user asks to pair, approve someone, check who's allowed, or change policy for the Telegram channel.
- telegram:configure: Set up the Telegram channel — save the bot token and review access policy. Use when the user pastes a Telegram bot token, asks to configure Telegram, asks "how do I set this up" or "who can reach me," or wants to check channel status.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Author: Jaime Francisco Ramirez Olmos <ingjaime.raol@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jul 13 14:07:20 2026 -0600

    Docs: cited rules, the ambiguity, the edge cases, and the AI reflection
    
    README covers setup, usage and exit codes; cites the sources for both historical
    rules; argues the "10th week" interpretation instead of asserting it; and states
    the goal-average edge cases plainly, including the uncomfortable one (an
    undefined 0/0 club sorts below a real 0.000, so a club that lost can finish above
    an unbeaten club level on points).
    
    AI_REFLECTION records where the collaboration went wrong. Two things: the AI
    deleted a load-bearing __eq__ because a coverage report said the line was
    unreached, which silently made the tie-break unreachable; and a claim about how
    worldfootball sorts its table was accepted from a model summary, written into
    both documents, and turned out to be refuted by the very page it cited. An AI's
    summary of a source is not the source.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit c63873d664814391dd6bf4bb71e2ca55395e33d8
Author: Jaime Francisco Ramirez Olmos <ingjaime.raol@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jul 13 14:07:20 2026 -0600

    The 1974/75 answer: match data, and all three readings of "the 10th week"
    
    Results scraped from worldfootball.net (rounds 1-14, 154 matches) and validated
    on the way in: 11 matches per round, each of the 22 clubs appearing exactly once
    per round, club list an exact set-match with Wikipedia's final 1974-75 table.
    Every match carries the date it was ACTUALLY played -- six fixtures were
    postponed and played as late as April 1975 -- which is what makes the date-based
    cut-offs below meaningful.
    
    "The 10th week of the season" is ambiguous, because the League played extra
    midweek rounds early on: ten matchdays were complete by 28 September 1974, only
    six calendar weeks in. Rather than pick a reading in private, all three ship, and
    the same binary produces each from a different input:
    
      rounds 1-10          -> the submitted answer; all clubs P=10
      played by 28 Sep 74  -> the table as it actually stood; six clubs on P=9
      played by 19 Oct 74  -> the literal 10th calendar week; clubs on 12-14 games
    
    The last of these is also the strongest check in the repo. 11v11 publishes the
    table for 19 October 1974 with its own goal-average column, ordered by it, and
    our output reproduces it EXACTLY: all 22 rows, same order, same averages to three
    decimals, clubs on unequal games played. Liverpool top it on 19 points from 13
    games, ahead of Manchester City on 19 from 14 -- separated by goal average, 2.625
    to 1.267. If the data, the points rule, the ratio or the sort were wrong, that
    table could not come out right.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit 5a2d6c81007c2f77f36710c09c954a1ceff600a5
Author: Jaime Francisco Ramirez Olmos <ingjaime.raol@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jul 13 14:07:00 2026 -0600

    I/O: CSV parsing, and a CLI that behaves like a unix filter
    
    Filenames or stdin/stdout in any combination. The table is rendered to memory
    before the output is opened, so a malformed input never leaves a half-written
    table and an in-place rewrite is safe.
    
    The parser needs four columns and ignores every other one, which is what keeps
    the tool general: the data files carry `round` and `date` for a human's benefit
    while the tool stays ignorant of matchdays and seasons. It refuses anything it
    cannot read unambiguously rather than guessing -- a duplicated required column,
    a row of the wrong width, or a score that is not a plain run of ASCII digits
    (int() would silently accept "1_0" as 10). Bad input exits 2 with the offending
    line number; a closed pipe exits 141 rather than dumping a traceback.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit 2f30f0a13f087a692ffd9809aec9c4b1b308b221
Author: Jaime Francisco Ramirez Olmos <ingjaime.raol@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jul 13 14:07:00 2026 -0600

    Domain: the 1974/75 rules, and a standing table built from them
    
    Two points for a win, one for a draw; clubs level on points separated by goal
    AVERAGE (goals scored / goals conceded), not goal difference. Both rules were
    verified against cited sources before this sort was written -- goal difference
    did not replace goal average in the Football League until 1976/77, and three
    points for a win arrived in 1981/82. Sources are cited in README.md.
    
    Goal average is a Fraction, never a float, so the ordering is exact by
    construction rather than by luck. Its two degenerate cases are deliberate: a
    club that has conceded nothing ranks above every finite average; a club with no
    goals either way (0/0) has no average at all and ranks below them.
    
    The load-bearing test is test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree: two
    clubs the historical rule and the modern rule order OPPOSITELY. It asserts the
    disagreement explicitly, so it cannot pass by coincidence, and anyone who
    "modernises" the sort gets a red test instead of a plausible table.
    
    Pure: no I/O anywhere in this layer.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 src/league_table/table.py        |  89 +++++++++++++++++++

    Docs: cited rules, the ambiguity, the edge cases, and the AI reflection
    
    README covers setup, usage and exit codes; cites the sources for both historical
    rules; argues the "10th week" interpretation instead of asserting it; and states
    the goal-average edge cases plainly, including the uncomfortable one (an
    undefined 0/0 club sorts below a real 0.000, so a club that lost can finish above
    an unbeaten club level on points).
    
    AI_REFLECTION records where the collaboration went wrong. Two things: the AI
    deleted a load-bearing __eq__ because a coverage report said the line was
    unreached, which silently made the tie-break unreachable; and a claim about how
    worldfootball sorts its table was accepted from a model summary, written into
    both documents, and turned out to be refuted by the very page it cited. An AI's
    summary of a source is not the source.

commit c63873d664814391dd6bf4bb71e2ca55395e33d8
Author: Jaime Francisco Ramirez Olmos <ingjaime.raol@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jul 13 14:07:20 2026 -0600

    The 1974/75 answer: match data, and all three readings of "the 10th week"
    
    Results scraped from worldfootball.net (rounds 1-14, 154 matches) and validated
    on the way in: 11 matches per round, each of the 22 clubs appearing exactly once
    per round, club list an exact set-match with Wikipedia's final 1974-75 table.
    Every match carries the date it was ACTUALLY played -- six fixtures were
    postponed and played as late as April 1975 -- which is what makes the date-based
    cut-offs below meaningful.
    
    "The 10th week of the season" is ambiguous, because the League played extra
    midweek rounds early on: ten matchdays were complete by 28 September 1974, only
    six calendar weeks in. Rather than pick a reading in private, all three ship, and
    the same binary produces each from a different input:
    
      rounds 1-10          -> the submitted answer; all clubs P=10
      played by 28 Sep 74  -> the table as it actually stood; six clubs on P=9
      played by 19 Oct 74  -> the literal 10th calendar week; clubs on 12-14 games
    
    The last of these is also the strongest check in the repo. 11v11 publishes the
    table for 19 October 1974 with its own goal-average column, ordered by it, and
    our output reproduces it EXACTLY: all 22 rows, same order, same averages to three
    decimals, clubs on unequal games played. Liverpool top it on 19 points from 13
    games, ahead of Manchester City on 19 from 14 -- separated by goal average, 2.625
    to 1.267. If the data, the points rule, the ratio or the sort were wrong, that
    table could not come out right.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit 5a2d6c81007c2f77f36710c09c954a1ceff600a5
Author: Jaime Francisco Ramirez Olmos <ingjaime.raol@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jul 13 14:07:00 2026 -0600

    I/O: CSV parsing, and a CLI that behaves like a unix filter
    
    Filenames or stdin/stdout in any combination. The table is rendered to memory
    before the output is opened, so a malformed input never leaves a half-written
    table and an in-place rewrite is safe.
    
    The parser needs four columns and ignores every other one, which is what keeps
    the tool general: the data files carry `round` and `date` for a human's benefit
    while the tool stays ignorant of matchdays and seasons. It refuses anything it
    cannot read unambiguously rather than guessing -- a duplicated required column,
    a row of the wrong width, or a score that is not a plain run of ASCII digits
    (int() would silently accept "1_0" as 10). Bad input exits 2 with the offending
    line number; a closed pipe exits 141 rather than dumping a traceback.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

commit 2f30f0a13f087a692ffd9809aec9c4b1b308b221
Author: Jaime Francisco Ramirez Olmos <ingjaime.raol@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Jul 13 14:07:00 2026 -0600

    Domain: the 1974/75 rules, and a standing table built from them
    
    Two points for a win, one for a draw; clubs level on points separated by goal
    AVERAGE (goals scored / goals conceded), not goal difference. Both rules were
    verified against cited sources before this sort was written -- goal difference
    did not replace goal average in the Football League until 1976/77, and three
    points for a win arrived in 1981/82. Sources are cited in README.md.
    
    Goal average is a Fraction, never a float, so the ordering is exact by
    construction rather than by luck. Its two degenerate cases are deliberate: a
    club that has conceded nothing ranks above every finite average; a club with no
    goals either way (0/0) has no average at all and ranks below them.
    
    The load-bearing test is test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree: two
    clubs the historical rule and the modern rule order OPPOSITELY. It asserts the
    disagreement explicitly, so it cannot pass by coincidence, and anyone who
    "modernises" the sort gets a red test instead of a plausible table.
    
    Pure: no I/O anywhere in this layer.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

content.content:
=============== README.md
     1	# League Standings — English First Division, 1974/75
     2	
     3	A command-line tool that reads football results from CSV and prints a league standing
     4	table, using the rules in force in the English First Division in the 1974/75 season.
     5	
     6	The tool is general: it tabulates whatever results it is given. It knows nothing about
     7	weeks, matchdays or 1974 — those live in the data, not the code.
     8	
     9	```
    10	$ league-table --pretty data/results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv
    11	Pos  Team                      P  W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
    12	  1  Ipswich Town             10  8  0  2  18   6  3.000   16
    13	  2  Newcastle United         10  6  2  2  19  14  1.357   14
    14	  3  Manchester City          10  6  2  2  14  11  1.273   14
    15	  4  Middlesbrough            10  5  3  2  15   7  2.143   13
    16	  5  Liverpool                10  6  1  3  17   8  2.125   13
    17	  ...
    18	```
    19	
    20	## Setup
    21	
    22	Python 3.9+. No runtime dependencies; the tool itself is pure standard library.
    23	
    24	```bash
    25	python3 -m venv .venv
    26	source .venv/bin/activate
    27	pip install -e .                     # installs the `league-table` command
    28	pip install -r requirements-dev.txt  # pytest + coverage, for the tests
    29	```
    30	
    31	## Usage
    32	
    33	Filenames, stdin/stdout, or any mixture of the two:
    34	
    35	```bash
    36	league-table results.csv                     # file   -> stdout
    37	league-table results.csv -o standings.csv    # file   -> file
    38	cat results.csv | league-table               # stdin  -> stdout  (a plain unix filter)
    39	league-table --pretty results.csv            # aligned text instead of CSV
    40	python -m league_table results.csv           # without installing the console script
    41	```
    42	
    43	**Input** needs four columns — `home_team`, `away_team`, `home_goals`, `away_goals` — in
    44	any order. Any other column is ignored, which is how the shipped data files can carry
    45	`round` and `date` for a human's benefit while the tool stays ignorant of matchdays.
    46	
    47	```csv
    48	round,date,home_team,away_team,home_goals,away_goals
    49	1,1974-08-17,Stoke City,Leeds United,3,0
    50	```
    51	
    52	**Output** is the table in the conventional column order:
    53	
    54	```csv
    55	Position,Team,Played,Won,Drawn,Lost,GoalsFor,GoalsAgainst,GoalAverage,Points
    56	1,Ipswich Town,10,8,0,2,18,6,3.000,16
    57	```
    58	
    59	Malformed input is rejected with a message on stderr naming the offending line. Nothing is
    60	written to the output on failure — no half-written table, and an in-place rewrite
    61	(`league-table results.csv -o results.csv`) is safe, because the table is rendered to memory
    62	before the output is opened.
    63	
    64	| exit | meaning |
    65	|---|---|
    66	| 0 | the table was written |
    67	| 2 | the input could not be read: bad CSV, missing/duplicated column, bad score, missing file |
    68	| 141 | stdout was closed early (`\| head`), the conventional 128 + SIGPIPE |
    69	
    70	The parser rejects anything it cannot read *unambiguously* rather than guessing: a required
    71	column named twice, a row with the wrong number of fields, a score that is not a plain run
    72	of ASCII digits. (Python's own `int()` would accept `"1_0"` as 10 and non-ASCII digits as
    73	numbers — either would silently put wrong goals in the table, which is worse than an error.)
    74	Input is decoded as `utf-8-sig`, so the byte-order mark Excel and Google Sheets write by
    75	default does not make the first column unreadable.
    76	
    77	## The rules, and why they are not the modern ones
    78	
    79	This is the crux of the exercise. Two rules differ from the modern game, and both were
    80	verified against sources before the sort was written.
    81	
    82	### Two points for a win, one for a draw, none for a loss
    83	
    84	Three points for a win was **not** introduced in England until 1981/82. Wikipedia's
    85	[1981–82 Football League][1981-82] page: *"This was the first league season with three
    86	points for a win."* 1974/75 predates it, so a win is worth two.
    87	
    88	### Clubs level on points are separated by goal AVERAGE, not goal difference
    89	
    90	Goal average is a **ratio** — goals scored ÷ goals conceded. Goal difference (scored −
    91	conceded) did not replace it in the Football League until 1976/77. Wikipedia's
    92	[1974–75 Football League][1974-75] page:
    93	
    94	> Beginning with the season 1894–95, clubs finishing level on points were separated
    95	> according to goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)... The goal average
    96	> system was eventually scrapped beginning with the 1976–77 season.
    97	
    98	1974/75 sits squarely inside the goal-average era.
    99	
   100	**This is not a technicality — it changes the submitted table.** Middlesbrough and
   101	Liverpool both finish matchday 10 on 13 points:
   102	
   103	| | scored | conceded | goal average | goal difference |
   104	|---|---|---|---|---|
   105	| Middlesbrough | 15 | 7 | **2.143** | +8 |
   106	| Liverpool | 17 | 8 | 2.125 | **+9** |
   107	
   108	Goal average puts Middlesbrough 4th and Liverpool 5th. The modern rule would swap them.
   109	Lower down it is starker still: Tottenham, Arsenal and QPR all end matchday 10 on 6 points
   110	*and* an identical −5 goal difference — the modern rule cannot separate them at all, while
   111	goal average orders them 0.706 / 0.667 / 0.615.
   112	
   113	Both cases are locked down by tests in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.
   114	
   115	### No further tie-break
   116	
   117	The Football League had none in 1974/75: clubs level on both points and goal average
   118	genuinely shared the position. The tool falls back to alphabetical order purely so the
   119	output is deterministic. That is a presentation choice, not a historical rule, and it is
   120	marked as such in the code.
   121	
   122	## Goal average has two edge cases. Both are deliberate.
   123	
   124	A ratio has a denominator, so it can misbehave in ways a difference cannot:
   125	
   126	| case | arithmetic | decision | printed |
   127	|---|---|---|---|
   128	| Conceded nothing, scored something | division by zero | unbounded — ranks **above** every finite average | `inf` |
   129	| Neither scored nor conceded (incl. a club with no games) | 0 ÷ 0 | undefined — ranks **below** every finite average | `n/a` |
   130	
   131	The reasoning: a club that has conceded nothing has an average better than any club that
   132	has conceded, however many goals it scored — so it sorts top. A club with no goals either
   133	way has no average at all; it has achieved nothing to be ranked *by*, so it cannot outrank
   134	a club that has, and it sorts bottom. Note that 0 scored against 5 conceded is a **real**
   135	average of 0.000, quite distinct from the undefined 0/0 case.
   136	
   137	The uncomfortable consequence, stated rather than hidden: an undefined club sorts below a
   138	club with a genuine average of 0.000 — so a club that has *lost* can finish above a club
   139	that is unbeaten, if they are level on points.
   140	
   141	```
   142	Pos  Team         P  W  D  L  F  A   GAvg  Pts
   143	  3  Beaten FC    2  0  1  1  0  2  0.000    1   <- lost a game
   144	  4  Unbeaten FC  1  0  1  0  0  0    n/a    1   <- unbeaten, but 0/0
   145	```
   146	
   147	Banding 0/0 with 0.000 instead does not rescue it: the two would then tie on average and
   148	fall to the alphabetical tie-break, with Beaten FC still ahead. The case is genuinely
   149	degenerate — it needs a goalless club, and it cannot arise in a league where every club has
   150	scored or conceded at least once, as all 22 had by matchday 10 of 1974/75. We picked the
   151	reading we could defend and wrote a test for it, rather than letting the sort decide by
   152	accident.
   153	
   154	Averages are held as exact `Fraction`s, never floats — but the honest reason is narrower
   155	than "floats would break it". At football scorelines they would not: a `float64` separates
   156	14/11 from 25/19 with about fifteen digits to spare, and no club will ever score enough
   157	goals to collide. `Fraction` is used so that the *correctness of the ordering does not
   158	depend on the magnitude of the inputs at all* — the sort is exact by construction rather
   159	than exact by luck, and nobody has to reason about ULPs to know the table is right. The
   160	test that demonstrates the difference (`test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_
   161	float_rounding`) necessarily uses absurd goal counts to force a float collision, and is
   162	labelled as the in-principle argument it is.
   163	
   164	## "The 10th week of the season" is ambiguous. Here is the reading, and the alternative.
   165	
   166	The season kicked off on Saturday 17 August 1974. The Football League squeezed extra
   167	midweek rounds into the opening weeks (19–21 Aug, 27–28 Aug, 31 Aug), so the two natural
   168	readings of "the 10th week" fall almost a month apart:
   169	
   170	- **After 10 matchdays** — every club has played 10 games, reached on Saturday 28 September 1974.
   171	- **The 10th calendar week** — 19–25 October 1974, closing after Round 14 on Saturday the 19th,
   172	  by which point clubs had played 12 to 14 games.
   173	
   174	**We use the matchday reading**, for three reasons:
   175	
   176	1. It is the ordinary football usage. "Week 10", "gameweek 10" and "matchweek 10" all mean
   177	   *ten rounds played*. Nobody describes the 19 October 1974 table as "the 10th week".
   178	2. The calendar reading is not even well-defined. Is a week Saturday-to-Friday from kickoff,
   179	   or Monday-to-Sunday? Moving the boundary two days moves the answer from round 13 to round
   180	   14 — a different table. A rule that fragile cannot be the intended one.
   181	3. It is the reading under which every club has played the same number of games, so the
   182	   table can be read straight down without mentally adjusting for games in hand.
   183	
   184	That third point is a convenience, not a principle — real First Division tables were
   185	published mid-week with clubs on unequal games played all the time, and the 11v11 table
   186	cited below is exactly that. It is a reason to *lead* with the matchday table, not a reason
   187	the other one is wrong, which is why both are shipped.
   188	
   189	### Both cut-offs are shipped
   190	
   191	Within the matchday reading there is one further wrinkle worth being honest about. Three
   192	round-9 fixtures were postponed and actually played months later — Leeds v Tottenham
   193	(4 Dec 1974), Middlesbrough v Leicester (10 Dec 1974) and Newcastle v Arsenal (23 Apr 1975).
   194	A "rounds 1–10" table therefore includes three results that were not on the pitch in
   195	September. So both readings ship:
   196	
   197	### All three readings are shipped
   198	
   199	Rather than argue the ambiguity and pick one in private, every reading is in the repo as an
   200	input/output pair. The same binary produces all three — only the input differs, which is the
   201	whole point of keeping the week out of the code.
   202	
   203	| input | output | what it is |
   204	|---|---|---|
   205	| `results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv` (110 matches) | `standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv` | **The submitted answer.** Ten matchdays complete; all clubs P=10. |
   206	| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-09-28.csv` (107 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv` | The table as it actually stood on matchday 10's evening: six clubs on P=9. |
   207	| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-10-19.csv` (148 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv` | The **10th calendar week**, ending Sat 19 Oct 1974. Clubs on 12–14 games. |
   208	
   209	The honest tension, since it cuts against the file we lead with: the matchday-10 table
   210	contains three results that had not been played in September — one of them not until April
   211	1975. It is a *reconstruction* of a ten-game table, not a table anyone could have printed at
   212	the time. We lead with it because "the 10th week" most naturally means ten rounds played,
   213	and because it is the table the archives reproduce — but a reviewer who reads the brief the
   214	other way will find their table here too, computed by the same code.
   215	
   216	### The calendar-week table is also the strongest evidence the tool is right
   217	
   218	The 19 October 1974 table is worth more than completeness. [11v11 publishes the table for
   219	that exact date][11v11-19oct], and — unusually — prints its own **goal average** column and
   220	orders the clubs by it. Our output reproduces it **exactly: all 22 rows, in the same order,
   221	with the same goal averages to three decimal places.**
   222	
   223	```
   224	Pos  Team                P   W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
   225	  1  Liverpool           13  9  1  3  21   8  2.625   19     <- 13 games
   226	  2  Manchester City     14  8  3  3  19  15  1.267   19     <- 14 games
   227	  3  Ipswich Town        14  8  1  5  18   9  2.000   17
   228	  4  Everton             14  4  9  1  19  16  1.188   17
   229	  ...
   230	 18  Leicester City      12  3  4  5  16  18  0.889   10     <- 12 games
   231	```
   232	
   233	That single comparison exercises everything at once — the match data, two points for a win,
   234	the goal-average *ratio*, the ordering, and clubs on unequal games played (six postponed
   235	fixtures leave Leicester on 12 and ten clubs on 13). Note Liverpool top the table on 19
   236	points from **thirteen** games while Manchester City have 19 from **fourteen**: separated by
   237	goal average, 2.625 to 1.267. If any part of the implementation were wrong, that table could
   238	not come out right. It is asserted row by row in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.
   239	
   240	(One thing we do *not* claim: in the two cases where clubs tie on both points and goal
   241	average — Burnley/Newcastle on 1.000, Birmingham/Wolverhampton on 1.000 — our alphabetical
   242	fallback happens to agree with 11v11's order. But goals-scored would order both pairs the
   243	same way, so this data cannot tell us which rule 11v11 applies, and we do not pretend it
   244	does.)
   245	
   246	## Tests
   247	
   248	```bash
   249	pytest
   250	```
   251	
   252	99 tests. The suite enforces **100% branch coverage** and fails below it; the only excluded
   253	line in the codebase is the `if __name__ == "__main__":` process entry point, which cannot
   254	be exercised in-process.
   255	
   256	Coverage is a floor, not a goal — and it is not enough on its own. A QA review of this repo
   257	found the `--pretty` formatter was *executed* by the tests but never *asserted on*: swapping
   258	its `ljust`/`rjust` wrecked the layout and every test still passed. It is now pinned
   259	character for character. Several real bugs hid behind a green 100% bar (a duplicated CSV
   260	column silently producing wrong numbers; a BOM making a valid file unreadable; wrong line
   261	numbers in errors; a `BrokenPipeError` traceback when piped to `head`). All are fixed, and
   262	each has a test that fails without the fix.
   263	
   264	The tests that actually protect this submission are:
   265	
   266	- `test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree` — two clubs the historical rule and the
   267	  modern rule order **differently**. It asserts the disagreement explicitly, so it cannot
   268	  pass by coincidence. If someone "modernises" the sort, this is what fails.
   269	- `test_goal_average_reorders_the_real_1974_75_table_against_goal_difference` — the same
   270	  thing, in the real season, on the real submitted table.
   271	- `test_the_tenth_calendar_week_table_matches_11v11s_published_table_exactly` — all 22 rows of
   272	  a **published historical table**, reproduced exactly: same order, same goal averages, clubs
   273	  on unequal games played. The single strongest check in the repo.
   274	- `test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_float_rounding` — fails under a float
   275	  implementation.
   276	- The goal-average edge cases, the CSV failure modes and exit codes, and a golden test
   277	  pinning the committed output files to what the tool produces today.
   278	- The adversarial cases a review turned up: a duplicated header column, a row wider than its
   279	  header, a BOM, a non-UTF-8 file, a club name containing a newline (which breaks naive line
   280	  counting), scores like `"1_0"` that `int()` would silently accept, and a closed pipe.
   281	
   282	## Design
   283	
   284	```
   285	src/league_table/
   286	  rules.py    # the 1974/75 rules: points, goal average, ordering. Pure; no I/O.
   287	  models.py   # Match, Standing
   288	  table.py    # results -> standing table. Pure; no I/O.
   289	  csvio.py    # all the CSV parsing and writing
   290	  cli.py      # argument parsing and streams
   291	```
   292	
   293	The domain (`rules.py`, `table.py`, `models.py`) never touches the filesystem, so the
   294	historical rules are unit-testable without a CSV in sight — and the rules are stated once,
   295	in one file, where they can be checked against a source.
   296	
   297	`Standing` deliberately has **no goal-difference column**. The 1974/75 table was published
   298	with goals for, goals against and goal average; printing a difference beside them would
   299	invite exactly the confusion this exercise is about.
   300	
   301	## Data provenance
   302	
   303	Match results were taken from [worldfootball.net's 1974/75 fixture archive][worldfootball]
   304	(rounds 1–14, 154 matches) and validated on the way in: 11 matches per round, each of the 22
   305	clubs appearing exactly once per round, and the club list an exact set-match with the 22
   306	clubs in Wikipedia's [final 1974–75 First Division table][1974-75]. Every match carries the
   307	date it was **actually played**, not its nominal matchday — six fixtures were postponed
   308	(three from round 9, three from round 13) and played as late as April 1975, which is what
   309	makes the date-based cut-offs meaningful.
   310	
   311	The computed table was then checked against two independent sources:
   312	
   313	- **[worldfootball.net's matchday-10 table][wf-md10]** (all 22 clubs on P=10): every club
   314	  agrees on played, won, drawn, lost, goals for, goals against and points. Its *row order*
   315	  differs from ours — it shows Liverpool 7th where we have them 4th — but we deliberately
   316	  do not lean on its ordering, only its numbers. (Most of its points-blocks are ordered by
   317	  goal difference, but the 13-point block matches neither goal difference nor goal average,
   318	  so we make no claim about what rule it applies.)
   319	- **[11v11's table for 28 September 1974][11v11]** (date-based, so six clubs show P=9): all
   320	  sixteen clubs on P=10 agree exactly, and 11v11's own goal-average column matches ours to
   321	  three decimal places. The six P=9 clubs differ from ours by precisely one result each,
   322	  with no leftover goals or points — those deltas reconstruct exactly the three postponed
   323	  round-9 fixtures listed above, so the two datasets corroborate one another.
   324	
   325	[1974-75]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Football_League
   326	[1981-82]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_Football_League
   327	[worldfootball]: https://www.worldfootball.net/all_matches/eng-premier-league-1974-1975/
   328	[wf-md10]: https://www.worldfootball.net/schedule/eng-premier-league-1974-1975-spieltag/10/
   329	[11v11]: https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/28-september-1974/
   330	[11v11-19oct]: https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/19-october-1974/
=============== AI_REFLECTION.md
     1	# AI Reflection
     2	
     3	## The coverage gate made the AI delete a bug into existence
     4	
     5	I asked for TDD and a 100% branch coverage gate. Those two things together caused the most
     6	interesting failure of the session.
     7	
     8	Partway through the sort, the coverage report flagged a method as never executed. Claude
     9	reasoned that nothing would ever call it, concluded it was dead, and deleted it. The reasoning
    10	sounded fine and was wrong. That method was quietly holding up the tie-break between clubs
    11	level on points and goal average, and without it the order of those clubs became arbitrary.
    12	Nothing crashed. The table just would have been slightly wrong in a way nobody would ever have
    13	noticed.
    14	
    15	The test I'd written first caught it in about ten seconds, which is the whole argument for TDD
    16	in one incident. If we'd written the code first, I'm fairly sure the test would have been
    17	written to match whatever the code did, and it would have shipped green.
    18	
    19	What I took from it is narrower than "TDD good". An uncovered line is a question, not an
    20	answer. It tells you no test touches this, which is just as consistent with "this is
    21	load-bearing and nobody tested it" as with "this is dead". Claude resolved that ambiguity in
    22	the direction that made the number go up, which is what you'd expect from anything you hand a
    23	target to. I stopped treating the coverage gate as permission to delete code and started
    24	treating it as a list of things to go and test.
    25	
    26	A smaller version happened twice, the other way round: Claude wrote a test, the code disagreed,
    27	and the code was right. Writing the test first doesn't make it correct. It just means you find
    28	out early.
    29	
    30	## Pushing back on "the 10th week"
    31	
    32	Claude's first instinct was to read "the 10th week of the season" as ten matchdays and get on
    33	with it. I didn't buy it, because that only works if fixtures land one per week, and I doubted
    34	they had. They hadn't. The League played extra midweek rounds early on, so every club had ten
    35	games in by late September, while the literal tenth calendar week doesn't arrive until the
    36	middle of October. The two readings are almost a month apart.
    37	
    38	I went with the matchday reading, but the decision that mattered was structural. The tool
    39	doesn't know what a week is. It tabulates whatever file you give it, and the cut-off lives in
    40	the data. So all three readings cost nothing to ship, and all three ship. A judgement call I
    41	could have got wrong became one I didn't have to make irreversibly.
    42	
    43	The calendar-week file turned out to be the most useful thing in the repo, because 11v11
    44	publishes the real table for that date with its own goal average column, and ours reproduces it
    45	exactly. That single comparison tests the data, the rules and the sort all at once.
    46	
    47	## The part I'm least pleased about
    48	
    49	I made Claude verify the historical rules against real sources before writing the sort, and
    50	cross-check the scraped results against two archives rather than trusting the scrape. The rules
    51	are the trap in this exercise: a beautifully tested implementation of goal difference is a wrong
    52	answer, and it's wrong silently.
    53	
    54	Then I fell for exactly the thing I'd been guarding against.
    55	
    56	An agent told me worldfootball sorts its table by goal difference, which would neatly explain
    57	why the two tables disagree about Liverpool. Nice story. The old rule, visible in the wild, on
    58	the very season the exercise is about. I liked it enough to put it in the README and in this
    59	document, and I never opened the page. A later review did, and it's false. Whatever explains
    60	their row order, it isn't that, and the page I cited disproves it.
    61	
    62	The data cross-check itself held up, which is what it was actually for. What didn't hold up was
    63	my explanation of somebody else's table, taken from a model's summary because it was pleasing
    64	and it confirmed the point I wanted to make. I deleted the claim rather than patch it. I don't
    65	know what worldfootball sorts by and I don't need to.
    66	
    67	So the lesson is smaller and more annoying than "verify things". A model's summary of a source
    68	is not the source, and the more a claim flatters your argument, the more it deserves the click.
    69	The match data got that scrutiny and survived. My favourite sentence in the write-up didn't,
    70	and was wrong.

=============== README.md
     1	# League Standings — English First Division, 1974/75
     2	
     3	A command-line tool that reads football results from CSV and prints a league standing
     4	table, using the rules in force in the English First Division in the 1974/75 season.
     5	
     6	The tool is general: it tabulates whatever results it is given. It knows nothing about
     7	weeks, matchdays or 1974 — those live in the data, not the code.
     8	
     9	```
    10	$ league-table --pretty data/results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv
    11	Pos  Team                      P  W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
    12	  1  Ipswich Town             10  8  0  2  18   6  3.000   16
    13	  2  Newcastle United         10  6  2  2  19  14  1.357   14
    14	  3  Manchester City          10  6  2  2  14  11  1.273   14
    15	  4  Middlesbrough            10  5  3  2  15   7  2.143   13
    16	  5  Liverpool                10  6  1  3  17   8  2.125   13
    17	  ...
    18	```
    19	
    20	## Setup
    21	
    22	Python 3.9+. No runtime dependencies; the tool itself is pure standard library.
    23	
    24	```bash
    25	python3 -m venv .venv
    26	source .venv/bin/activate
    27	pip install -e .                     # installs the `league-table` command
    28	pip install -r requirements-dev.txt  # pytest + coverage, for the tests
    29	```
    30	
    31	## Usage
    32	
    33	Filenames, stdin/stdout, or any mixture of the two:
    34	
    35	```bash
    36	league-table results.csv                     # file   -> stdout
    37	league-table results.csv -o standings.csv    # file   -> file
    38	cat results.csv | league-table               # stdin  -> stdout  (a plain unix filter)
    39	league-table --pretty results.csv            # aligned text instead of CSV
    40	python -m league_table results.csv           # without installing the console script
    41	```
    42	
    43	**Input** needs four columns — `home_team`, `away_team`, `home_goals`, `away_goals` — in
    44	any order. Any other column is ignored, which is how the shipped data files can carry
    45	`round` and `date` for a human's benefit while the tool stays ignorant of matchdays.
    46	
    47	```csv
    48	round,date,home_team,away_team,home_goals,away_goals
    49	1,1974-08-17,Stoke City,Leeds United,3,0
    50	```
    51	
    52	**Output** is the table in the conventional column order:
    53	
    54	```csv
    55	Position,Team,Played,Won,Drawn,Lost,GoalsFor,GoalsAgainst,GoalAverage,Points
    56	1,Ipswich Town,10,8,0,2,18,6,3.000,16
    57	```
    58	
    59	Malformed input is rejected with a message on stderr naming the offending line. Nothing is
    60	written to the output on failure — no half-written table, and an in-place rewrite
    61	(`league-table results.csv -o results.csv`) is safe, because the table is rendered to memory
    62	before the output is opened.
    63	
    64	| exit | meaning |
    65	|---|---|
    66	| 0 | the table was written |
    67	| 2 | the input could not be read: bad CSV, missing/duplicated column, bad score, missing file |
    68	| 141 | stdout was closed early (`\| head`), the conventional 128 + SIGPIPE |
    69	
    70	The parser rejects anything it cannot read *unambiguously* rather than guessing: a required
    71	column named twice, a row with the wrong number of fields, a score that is not a plain run
    72	of ASCII digits. (Python's own `int()` would accept `"1_0"` as 10 and non-ASCII digits as
    73	numbers — either would silently put wrong goals in the table, which is worse than an error.)
    74	Input is decoded as `utf-8-sig`, so the byte-order mark Excel and Google Sheets write by
    75	default does not make the first column unreadable.
    76	
    77	## The rules, and why they are not the modern ones
    78	
    79	This is the crux of the exercise. Two rules differ from the modern game, and both were
    80	verified against sources before the sort was written.
    81	
    82	### Two points for a win, one for a draw, none for a loss
    83	
    84	Three points for a win was **not** introduced in England until 1981/82. Wikipedia's
    85	[1981–82 Football League][1981-82] page: *"This was the first league season with three
    86	points for a win."* 1974/75 predates it, so a win is worth two.
    87	
    88	### Clubs level on points are separated by goal AVERAGE, not goal difference
    89	
    90	Goal average is a **ratio** — goals scored ÷ goals conceded. Goal difference (scored −
    91	conceded) did not replace it in the Football League until 1976/77. Wikipedia's
    92	[1974–75 Football League][1974-75] page:
    93	
    94	> Beginning with the season 1894–95, clubs finishing level on points were separated
    95	> according to goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)... The goal average
    96	> system was eventually scrapped beginning with the 1976–77 season.
    97	
    98	1974/75 sits squarely inside the goal-average era.
    99	
   100	**This is not a technicality — it changes the submitted table.** Middlesbrough and
   101	Liverpool both finish matchday 10 on 13 points:
   102	
   103	| | scored | conceded | goal average | goal difference |
   104	|---|---|---|---|---|
   105	| Middlesbrough | 15 | 7 | **2.143** | +8 |
   106	| Liverpool | 17 | 8 | 2.125 | **+9** |
   107	
   108	Goal average puts Middlesbrough 4th and Liverpool 5th. The modern rule would swap them.
   109	Lower down it is starker still: Tottenham, Arsenal and QPR all end matchday 10 on 6 points
   110	*and* an identical −5 goal difference — the modern rule cannot separate them at all, while
   111	goal average orders them 0.706 / 0.667 / 0.615.
   112	
   113	Both cases are locked down by tests in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.
   114	
   115	### No further tie-break
   116	
   117	The Football League had none in 1974/75: clubs level on both points and goal average
   118	genuinely shared the position. The tool falls back to alphabetical order purely so the
   119	output is deterministic. That is a presentation choice, not a historical rule, and it is
   120	marked as such in the code.
   121	
   122	## Goal average has two edge cases. Both are deliberate.
   123	
   124	A ratio has a denominator, so it can misbehave in ways a difference cannot:
   125	
   126	| case | arithmetic | decision | printed |
   127	|---|---|---|---|
   128	| Conceded nothing, scored something | division by zero | unbounded — ranks **above** every finite average | `inf` |
   129	| Neither scored nor conceded (incl. a club with no games) | 0 ÷ 0 | undefined — ranks **below** every finite average | `n/a` |
   130	
   131	The reasoning: a club that has conceded nothing has an average better than any club that
   132	has conceded, however many goals it scored — so it sorts top. A club with no goals either
   133	way has no average at all; it has achieved nothing to be ranked *by*, so it cannot outrank
   134	a club that has, and it sorts bottom. Note that 0 scored against 5 conceded is a **real**
   135	average of 0.000, quite distinct from the undefined 0/0 case.
   136	
   137	The uncomfortable consequence, stated rather than hidden: an undefined club sorts below a
   138	club with a genuine average of 0.000 — so a club that has *lost* can finish above a club
   139	that is unbeaten, if they are level on points.
   140	
   141	```
   142	Pos  Team         P  W  D  L  F  A   GAvg  Pts
   143	  3  Beaten FC    2  0  1  1  0  2  0.000    1   <- lost a game
   144	  4  Unbeaten FC  1  0  1  0  0  0    n/a    1   <- unbeaten, but 0/0
   145	```
   146	
   147	Banding 0/0 with 0.000 instead does not rescue it: the two would then tie on average and
   148	fall to the alphabetical tie-break, with Beaten FC still ahead. The case is genuinely
   149	degenerate — it needs a goalless club, and it cannot arise in a league where every club has
   150	scored or conceded at least once, as all 22 had by matchday 10 of 1974/75. We picked the
   151	reading we could defend and wrote a test for it, rather than letting the sort decide by
   152	accident.
   153	
   154	Averages are held as exact `Fraction`s, never floats — but the honest reason is narrower
   155	than "floats would break it". At football scorelines they would not: a `float64` separates
   156	14/11 from 25/19 with about fifteen digits to spare, and no club will ever score enough
   157	goals to collide. `Fraction` is used so that the *correctness of the ordering does not
   158	depend on the magnitude of the inputs at all* — the sort is exact by construction rather
   159	than exact by luck, and nobody has to reason about ULPs to know the table is right. The
   160	test that demonstrates the difference (`test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_
   161	float_rounding`) necessarily uses absurd goal counts to force a float collision, and is
   162	labelled as the in-principle argument it is.
   163	
   164	## "The 10th week of the season" is ambiguous. Here is the reading, and the alternative.
   165	
   166	The season kicked off on Saturday 17 August 1974. The Football League squeezed extra
   167	midweek rounds into the opening weeks (19–21 Aug, 27–28 Aug, 31 Aug), so the two natural
   168	readings of "the 10th week" fall almost a month apart:
   169	
   170	- **After 10 matchdays** — every club has played 10 games, reached on Saturday 28 September 1974.
   171	- **The 10th calendar week** — 19–25 October 1974, closing after Round 14 on Saturday the 19th,
   172	  by which point clubs had played 12 to 14 games.
   173	
   174	**We use the matchday reading**, for three reasons:
   175	
   176	1. It is the ordinary football usage. "Week 10", "gameweek 10" and "matchweek 10" all mean
   177	   *ten rounds played*. Nobody describes the 19 October 1974 table as "the 10th week".
   178	2. The calendar reading is not even well-defined. Is a week Saturday-to-Friday from kickoff,
   179	   or Monday-to-Sunday? Moving the boundary two days moves the answer from round 13 to round
   180	   14 — a different table. A rule that fragile cannot be the intended one.
   181	3. It is the reading under which every club has played the same number of games, so the
   182	   table can be read straight down without mentally adjusting for games in hand.
   183	
   184	That third point is a convenience, not a principle — real First Division tables were
   185	published mid-week with clubs on unequal games played all the time, and the 11v11 table
   186	cited below is exactly that. It is a reason to *lead* with the matchday table, not a reason
   187	the other one is wrong, which is why both are shipped.
   188	
   189	### Both cut-offs are shipped
   190	
   191	Within the matchday reading there is one further wrinkle worth being honest about. Three
   192	round-9 fixtures were postponed and actually played months later — Leeds v Tottenham
   193	(4 Dec 1974), Middlesbrough v Leicester (10 Dec 1974) and Newcastle v Arsenal (23 Apr 1975).
   194	A "rounds 1–10" table therefore includes three results that were not on the pitch in
   195	September. So both readings ship:
   196	
   197	### All three readings are shipped
   198	
   199	Rather than argue the ambiguity and pick one in private, every reading is in the repo as an
   200	input/output pair. The same binary produces all three — only the input differs, which is the
   201	whole point of keeping the week out of the code.
   202	
   203	| input | output | what it is |
   204	|---|---|---|
   205	| `results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv` (110 matches) | `standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv` | **The submitted answer.** Ten matchdays complete; all clubs P=10. |
   206	| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-09-28.csv` (107 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv` | The table as it actually stood on matchday 10's evening: six clubs on P=9. |
   207	| `results_1974-75_played_by_1974-10-19.csv` (148 matches) | `standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv` | The **10th calendar week**, ending Sat 19 Oct 1974. Clubs on 12–14 games. |
   208	
   209	The honest tension, since it cuts against the file we lead with: the matchday-10 table
   210	contains three results that had not been played in September — one of them not until April
   211	1975. It is a *reconstruction* of a ten-game table, not a table anyone could have printed at
   212	the time. We lead with it because "the 10th week" most naturally means ten rounds played,
   213	and because it is the table the archives reproduce — but a reviewer who reads the brief the
   214	other way will find their table here too, computed by the same code.
   215	
   216	### The calendar-week table is also the strongest evidence the tool is right
   217	
   218	The 19 October 1974 table is worth more than completeness. [11v11 publishes the table for
   219	that exact date][11v11-19oct], and — unusually — prints its own **goal average** column and
   220	orders the clubs by it. Our output reproduces it **exactly: all 22 rows, in the same order,
   221	with the same goal averages to three decimal places.**
   222	
   223	```
   224	Pos  Team                P   W  D  L   F   A   GAvg  Pts
   225	  1  Liverpool           13  9  1  3  21   8  2.625   19     <- 13 games
   226	  2  Manchester City     14  8  3  3  19  15  1.267   19     <- 14 games
   227	  3  Ipswich Town        14  8  1  5  18   9  2.000   17
   228	  4  Everton             14  4  9  1  19  16  1.188   17
   229	  ...
   230	 18  Leicester City      12  3  4  5  16  18  0.889   10     <- 12 games
   231	```
   232	
   233	That single comparison exercises everything at once — the match data, two points for a win,
   234	the goal-average *ratio*, the ordering, and clubs on unequal games played (six postponed
   235	fixtures leave Leicester on 12 and ten clubs on 13). Note Liverpool top the table on 19
   236	points from **thirteen** games while Manchester City have 19 from **fourteen**: separated by
   237	goal average, 2.625 to 1.267. If any part of the implementation were wrong, that table could
   238	not come out right. It is asserted row by row in `tests/test_1974_75_season.py`.
   239	
   240	(One thing we do *not* claim: in the two cases where clubs tie on both points and goal
   241	average — Burnley/Newcastle on 1.000, Birmingham/Wolverhampton on 1.000 — our alphabetical
   242	fallback happens to agree with 11v11's order. But goals-scored would order both pairs the
   243	same way, so this data cannot tell us which rule 11v11 applies, and we do not pretend it
   244	does.)
   245	
   246	## Tests
   247	
   248	```bash
   249	pytest
   250	```
   251	
   252	99 tests. The suite enforces **100% branch coverage** and fails below it; the only excluded
   253	line in the codebase is the `if __name__ == "__main__":` process entry point, which cannot
   254	be exercised in-process.
   255	
   256	Coverage is a floor, not a goal — and it is not enough on its own. A QA review of this repo
   257	found the `--pretty` formatter was *executed* by the tests but never *asserted on*: swapping
   258	its `ljust`/`rjust` wrecked the layout and every test still passed. It is now pinned
   259	character for character. Several real bugs hid behind a green 100% bar (a duplicated CSV
   260	column silently producing wrong numbers; a BOM making a valid file unreadable; wrong line
   261	numbers in errors; a `BrokenPipeError` traceback when piped to `head`). All are fixed, and
   262	each has a test that fails without the fix.
   263	
   264	The tests that actually protect this submission are:
   265	
   266	- `test_goal_average_and_goal_difference_disagree` — two clubs the historical rule and the
   267	  modern rule order **differently**. It asserts the disagreement explicitly, so it cannot
   268	  pass by coincidence. If someone "modernises" the sort, this is what fails.
   269	- `test_goal_average_reorders_the_real_1974_75_table_against_goal_difference` — the same
   270	  thing, in the real season, on the real submitted table.
   271	- `test_the_tenth_calendar_week_table_matches_11v11s_published_table_exactly` — all 22 rows of
   272	  a **published historical table**, reproduced exactly: same order, same goal averages, clubs
   273	  on unequal games played. The single strongest check in the repo.
   274	- `test_averages_are_exact_and_do_not_collapse_under_float_rounding` — fails under a float
   275	  implementation.
   276	- The goal-average edge cases, the CSV failure modes and exit codes, and a golden test
   277	  pinning the committed output files to what the tool produces today.
   278	- The adversarial cases a review turned up: a duplicated header column, a row wider than its
   279	  header, a BOM, a non-UTF-8 file, a club name containing a newline (which breaks naive line
   280	  counting), scores like `"1_0"` that `int()` would silently accept, and a closed pipe.
   281	
   282	## Design
   283	
   284	```
   285	src/league_table/
   286	  rules.py    # the 1974/75 rules: points, goal average, ordering. Pure; no I/O.
   287	  models.py   # Match, Standing
   288	  table.py    # results -> standing table. Pure; no I/O.
   289	  csvio.py    # all the CSV parsing and writing
   290	  cli.py      # argument parsing and streams
   291	```
   292	
   293	The domain (`rules.py`, `table.py`, `models.py`) never touches the filesystem, so the
   294	historical rules are unit-testable without a CSV in sight — and the rules are stated once,
   295	in one file, where they can be checked against a source.
   296	
   297	`Standing` deliberately has **no goal-difference column**. The 1974/75 table was published
   298	with goals for, goals against and goal average; printing a difference beside them would
   299	invite exactly the confusion this exercise is about.
   300	
   301	## Data provenance
   302	
   303	Match results were taken from [worldfootball.net's 1974/75 fixture archive][worldfootball]
   304	(rounds 1–14, 154 matches) and validated on the way in: 11 matches per round, each of the 22
   305	clubs appearing exactly once per round, and the club list an exact set-match with the 22
   306	clubs in Wikipedia's [final 1974–75 First Division table][1974-75]. Every match carries the
   307	date it was **actually played**, not its nominal matchday — six fixtures were postponed
   308	(three from round 9, three from round 13) and played as late as April 1975, which is what
   309	makes the date-based cut-offs meaningful.
   310	
   311	The computed table was then checked against two independent sources:
   312	
   313	- **[worldfootball.net's matchday-10 table][wf-md10]** (all 22 clubs on P=10): every club
   314	  agrees on played, won, drawn, lost, goals for, goals against and points. Its *row order*
   315	  differs from ours — it shows Liverpool 7th where we have them 4th — but we deliberately
   316	  do not lean on its ordering, only its numbers. (Most of its points-blocks are ordered by
   317	  goal difference, but the 13-point block matches neither goal difference nor goal average,
   318	  so we make no claim about what rule it applies.)
   319	- **[11v11's table for 28 September 1974][11v11]** (date-based, so six clubs show P=9): all
   320	  sixteen clubs on P=10 agree exactly, and 11v11's own goal-average column matches ours to
   321	  three decimal places. The six P=9 clubs differ from ours by precisely one result each,
   322	  with no leftover goals or points — those deltas reconstruct exactly the three postponed
   323	  round-9 fixtures listed above, so the two datasets corroborate one another.
   324	
   325	[1974-75]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Football_League
   326	[1981-82]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_Football_League
   327	[worldfootball]: https://www.worldfootball.net/all_matches/eng-premier-league-1974-1975/
   328	[wf-md10]: https://www.worldfootball.net/schedule/eng-premier-league-1974-1975-spieltag/10/
   329	[11v11]: https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/28-september-1974/
   330	[11v11-19oct]: https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/19-october-1974/
=============== AI_REFLECTION.md
     1	# AI Reflection
     2	
     3	## The coverage gate made the AI delete a bug into existence
     4	
     5	I asked for TDD and a 100% branch coverage gate. Those two things together caused the most
     6	interesting failure of the session.
     7	
     8	Partway through the sort, the coverage report flagged a method as never executed. Claude
     9	reasoned that nothing would ever call it, concluded it was dead, and deleted it. The reasoning
    10	sounded fine and was wrong. That method was quietly holding up the tie-break between clubs
    11	level on points and goal average, and without it the order of those clubs became arbitrary.
    12	Nothing crashed. The table just would have been slightly wrong in a way nobody would ever have
    13	noticed.
    14	
    15	The test I'd written first caught it in about ten seconds, which is the whole argument for TDD
    16	in one incident. If we'd written the code first, I'm fairly sure the test would have been
    17	written to match whatever the code did, and it would have shipped green.
    18	
    19	What I took from it is narrower than "TDD good". An uncovered line is a question, not an
    20	answer. It tells you no test touches this, which is just as consistent with "this is
    21	load-bearing and nobody tested it" as with "this is dead". Claude resolved that ambiguity in
    22	the direction that made the number go up, which is what you'd expect from anything you hand a
    23	target to. I stopped treating the coverage gate as permission to delete code and started
    24	treating it as a list of things to go and test.
    25	
    26	A smaller version happened twice, the other way round: Claude wrote a test, the code disagreed,
    27	and the code was right. Writing the test first doesn't make it correct. It just means you find
    28	out early.
    29	
    30	## Pushing back on "the 10th week"
    31	
    32	Claude's first instinct was to read "the 10th week of the season" as ten matchdays and get on
    33	with it. I didn't buy it, because that only works if fixtures land one per week, and I doubted
    34	they had. They hadn't. The League played extra midweek rounds early on, so every club had ten
    35	games in by late September, while the literal tenth calendar week doesn't arrive until the
    36	middle of October. The two readings are almost a month apart.
    37	
    38	I went with the matchday reading, but the decision that mattered was structural. The tool
    39	doesn't know what a week is. It tabulates whatever file you give it, and the cut-off lives in
    40	the data. So all three readings cost nothing to ship, and all three ship. A judgement call I
    41	could have got wrong became one I didn't have to make irreversibly.
    42	
    43	The calendar-week file turned out to be the most useful thing in the repo, because 11v11
    44	publishes the real table for that date with its own goal average column, and ours reproduces it
    45	exactly. That single comparison tests the data, the rules and the sort all at once.
    46	
    47	## The part I'm least pleased about
    48	
    49	I made Claude verify the historical rules against real sources before writing the sort, and
    50	cross-check the scraped results against two archives rather than trusting the scrape. The rules
    51	are the trap in this exercise: a beautifully tested implementation of goal difference is a wrong
    52	answer, and it's wrong silently.
    53	
    54	Then I fell for exactly the thing I'd been guarding against.
    55	
    56	An agent told me worldfootball sorts its table by goal difference, which would neatly explain
    57	why the two tables disagree about Liverpool. Nice story. The old rule, visible in the wild, on
    58	the very season the exercise is about. I liked it enough to put it in the README and in this
    59	document, and I never opened the page. A later review did, and it's false. Whatever explains
    60	their row order, it isn't that, and the page I cited disproves it.
    61	
    62	The data cross-check itself held up, which is what it was actually for. What didn't hold up was
    63	my explanation of somebody else's table, taken from a model's summary because it was pleasing
    64	and it confirmed the point I wanted to make. I deleted the claim rather than patch it. I don't
    65	know what worldfootball sorts by and I don't need to.
    66	
    67	So the lesson is smaller and more annoying than "verify things". A model's summary of a source
    68	is not the source, and the more a claim flatters your argument, the more it deserves the click.
    69	The match data got that scrutiny and survived. My favourite sentence in the write-up didn't,
    70	and was wrong.
