=============== src/league_table/cli.py
     1	"""The command line. Argument parsing and streams; no rules live here.
     2	
     3	    league-table                       # stdin  -> stdout
     4	    league-table results.csv           # file   -> stdout
     5	    league-table results.csv -o t.csv  # file   -> file
     6	    cat results.csv | league-table     # a plain unix filter
     7	
     8	The table is computed in full before a single byte is written, so a malformed
     9	input never leaves a half-written table behind.
    10	"""
    11	
    12	import argparse
    13	import contextlib
    14	import io
    15	import os
    16	import sys
    17	from collections import Counter
    18	from typing import IO, Iterator, List, Optional, Sequence
    19	
    20	from league_table import __version__
    21	from league_table.csvio import InvalidInput, read_matches, write_table
    22	from league_table.models import Match, Standing
    23	from league_table.table import build_table
    24	
    25	EXIT_OK = 0
    26	EXIT_INVALID_INPUT = 2
    27	# The conventional status for a process killed by SIGPIPE (128 + 13), which is
    28	# what a shell would have reported had we not caught the error.
    29	EXIT_BROKEN_PIPE = 141
    30	
    31	_STDIO = "-"
    32	
    33	_PRETTY_COLUMNS = ("Pos", "Team", "P", "W", "D", "L", "F", "A", "GAvg", "Pts")
    34	
    35	
    36	def _parser() -> argparse.ArgumentParser:
    37	    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
    38	        prog="league-table",
    39	        description=(
    40	            "Calculate a football league standing table from match results, "
    41	            "using the rules of the English First Division in 1974/75: two points "
    42	            "for a win, one for a draw, and clubs level on points separated by "
    43	            "goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)."
    44	        ),
    45	    )
    46	    parser.add_argument(
    47	        "input",
    48	        nargs="?",
    49	        default=_STDIO,
    50	        help="CSV of results; omit or pass '-' to read standard input",
    51	    )
    52	    parser.add_argument(
    53	        "-o",
    54	        "--output",
    55	        default=_STDIO,
    56	        help="where to write the table; omit or pass '-' for standard output",
    57	    )
    58	    parser.add_argument(
    59	        "--pretty",
    60	        action="store_true",
    61	        help="print an aligned text table instead of CSV",
    62	    )
    63	    parser.add_argument(
    64	        "--version",
    65	        action="version",
    66	        version=f"league-table {__version__}",
    67	    )
    68	    return parser
    69	
    70	
    71	def _warn_about_repeated_fixtures(matches: Sequence[Match], stderr: IO[str]) -> None:
    72	    """Flag any pairing that appears more than once at the same ground.
    73	
    74	    Not an error: some leagues really do play the same fixture twice at the same
    75	    ground in a season, and this tool tabulates whatever league it is handed. But
    76	    a duplicated row in a results feed is the commonest way to end up with a
    77	    confidently wrong table, so it does not go through in silence.
    78	    """
    79	    played = Counter((match.home_team, match.away_team) for match in matches)
    80	
    81	    for (home, away), count in played.items():
    82	        if count > 1:
    83	            times = "twice" if count == 2 else f"{count} times"
    84	            print(
    85	                f"league-table: warning: {home} v {away} appears {times}; "
    86	                "every occurrence has been counted",
    87	                file=stderr,
    88	            )
    89	
    90	
    91	@contextlib.contextmanager
    92	def _opened(path: str, mode: str, stream: IO[str]) -> Iterator[IO[str]]:
    93	    """Yield the named file, or the given standard stream for '-'.
    94	
    95	    Input is decoded as utf-8-sig, which strips a byte order mark if one is
    96	    present and is a plain utf-8 read if not. Excel and Google Sheets both write
    97	    a BOM by default, so without this the commonest real-world CSV in existence
    98	    fails with a baffling "missing required column: home_team".
    99	    """
   100	    if path == _STDIO:
   101	        yield stream
   102	        return
   103	
   104	    encoding = "utf-8-sig" if mode == "r" else "utf-8"
   105	
   106	    try:
   107	        handle = open(path, mode, newline="", encoding=encoding)
   108	    except OSError as error:
   109	        raise InvalidInput(f"cannot open {path}: {error.strerror}") from None
   110	
   111	    try:
   112	        # A decode error is bad input, not a crash: UnicodeError is a ValueError,
   113	        # not an OSError, so it would otherwise sail past as a traceback.
   114	        yield handle
   115	    except UnicodeError as error:
   116	        raise InvalidInput(f"cannot read {path}: {error}") from None
   117	    finally:
   118	        handle.close()
   119	
   120	
   121	def _silence_stdout(stdout: IO[str]) -> None:
   122	    """Redirect the real stdout to /dev/null, if there is a real one.
   123	
   124	    Only meaningful for an actual file descriptor; under test the stream is an
   125	    in-memory buffer with nothing to redirect, so there is nothing to do.
   126	    """
   127	    try:
   128	        fileno = stdout.fileno()
   129	    except (AttributeError, io.UnsupportedOperation):
   130	        return
   131	
   132	    devnull = os.open(os.devnull, os.O_WRONLY)
   133	    os.dup2(devnull, fileno)
   134	
   135	
   136	def _write_pretty(standings: Sequence[Standing], stream: IO[str]) -> None:
   137	    rows = [
   138	        (
   139	            str(row.position),
   140	            row.team,
   141	            str(row.played),
   142	            str(row.won),
   143	            str(row.drawn),
   144	            str(row.lost),
   145	            str(row.goals_for),
   146	            str(row.goals_against),
   147	            str(row.goal_average),
   148	            str(row.points),
   149	        )
   150	        for row in standings
   151	    ]
   152	
   153	    widths = [
   154	        max(len(cell) for cell in column) for column in zip(_PRETTY_COLUMNS, *rows)
   155	    ]
   156	
   157	    def line(cells: Sequence[str]) -> str:
   158	        # The club name reads best left-aligned; the numbers line up right.
   159	        rendered = [
   160	            cell.ljust(width) if index == 1 else cell.rjust(width)
   161	            for index, (cell, width) in enumerate(zip(cells, widths))
   162	        ]
   163	        return "  ".join(rendered).rstrip()
   164	
   165	    stream.write(line(_PRETTY_COLUMNS) + "\n")
   166	    for row in rows:
   167	        stream.write(line(row) + "\n")
   168	
   169	
   170	def main(
   171	    argv: Optional[List[str]] = None,
   172	    stdin: Optional[IO[str]] = None,
   173	    stdout: Optional[IO[str]] = None,
   174	    stderr: Optional[IO[str]] = None,
   175	) -> int:
   176	    """Run the tool. Returns the process exit code.
   177	
   178	    The streams are injectable so the tests can drive the real entry point
   179	    without spawning a process or monkeypatching `sys`.
   180	    """
   181	    stdin = sys.stdin if stdin is None else stdin
   182	    stdout = sys.stdout if stdout is None else stdout
   183	    stderr = sys.stderr if stderr is None else stderr
   184	
   185	    args = _parser().parse_args(argv)
   186	
   187	    try:
   188	        with _opened(args.input, "r", stdin) as source:
   189	            matches = read_matches(source)
   190	
   191	        _warn_about_repeated_fixtures(matches, stderr)
   192	
   193	        standings = build_table(matches)
   194	
   195	        # Render to memory first: if anything above had failed we would not have
   196	        # touched the output at all, and this keeps that guarantee for the write.
   197	        rendered = io.StringIO()
   198	        if args.pretty:
   199	            _write_pretty(standings, rendered)
   200	        else:
   201	            write_table(standings, rendered)
   202	
   203	        with _opened(args.output, "w", stdout) as destination:
   204	            destination.write(rendered.getvalue())
   205	            # Flush while we can still do something about a failure. Left to the
   206	            # interpreter's exit-time flush, a broken pipe or a full disk would
   207	            # surface as a traceback and an exit code we never chose.
   208	            destination.flush()
   209	
   210	    except InvalidInput as error:
   211	        print(f"league-table: {error}", file=stderr)
   212	        return EXIT_INVALID_INPUT
   213	
   214	    except BrokenPipeError:
   215	        # `league-table results.csv | head -1` closes the pipe under us. That is
   216	        # a reader losing interest, not an error -- but Python flushes stdout
   217	        # again on the way out, which would raise a second BrokenPipeError and
   218	        # print a traceback. Point the fd at /dev/null so that final flush has
   219	        # somewhere harmless to go, and exit the way a unix filter should.
   220	        _silence_stdout(stdout)
   221	        return EXIT_BROKEN_PIPE
   222	
   223	    return EXIT_OK

     1	"""CSV in, CSV out. All the I/O lives here; none of the rules do.
     2	
     3	The parser needs four columns -- home_team, away_team, home_goals, away_goals --
     4	and ignores every other column it finds. That is what lets the data files carry
     5	`round` and `date` for a human's benefit while the tool stays entirely ignorant
     6	of matchdays and seasons.
     7	"""
     8	
     9	import csv
    10	from collections import Counter
    11	from typing import IO, Iterable, List, Sequence
    12	
    13	from league_table.models import Match, Standing
    14	
    15	REQUIRED_COLUMNS = ("home_team", "away_team", "home_goals", "away_goals")
    16	
    17	OUTPUT_COLUMNS = (
    18	    "Position",
    19	    "Team",
    20	    "Played",
    21	    "Won",
    22	    "Drawn",
    23	    "Lost",
    24	    "GoalsFor",
    25	    "GoalsAgainst",
    26	    "GoalAverage",
    27	    "Points",
    28	)
    29	
    30	
    31	class InvalidInput(Exception):
    32	    """The input is not a readable set of results.
    33	
    34	    Carries a message naming the offending line, so a human can go and fix it.
    35	    """
    36	
    37	
    38	def _normalise_header(fieldnames: Sequence[str]) -> List[str]:
    39	    # A leading BOM would otherwise ride along on the first column name, so that
    40	    # 'home_team' fails to match 'home_team'. Files opened by this module are
    41	    # decoded as utf-8-sig, but a caller can hand us any stream -- stdin, most
    42	    # obviously -- so strip it here too.
    43	    return [(name or "").lstrip("﻿").strip().lower() for name in fieldnames]
    44	
    45	
    46	def _is_goal_count(value: str) -> bool:
    47	    """True for a plain run of ASCII digits, and nothing else."""
    48	    return value.isascii() and value.isdigit()
    49	
    50	
    51	def _read_goals(row: dict, column: str, line: int) -> int:
    52	    # Every required column is guaranteed present: the header is checked once up
    53	    # front, and rows of the wrong width are rejected before we get here.
    54	    value = row[column].strip()
    55	
    56	    if value == "":
    57	        raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: missing a value for '{column}'")
    58	
    59	    if value.startswith("-") and _is_goal_count(value[1:]):
    60	        raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: '{column}' cannot be negative, got {value}")
    61	
    62	    # Deliberately stricter than int(), which accepts PEP 515 underscores
    63	    # ("1_0" -> 10) and non-ASCII decimal digits. Neither is a goal count, and
    64	    # both would silently produce a wrong table rather than an error.
    65	    if not _is_goal_count(value):
    66	        raise InvalidInput(
    67	            f"line {line}: '{column}' must be a whole number of goals, got {value!r}"
    68	        )
    69	
    70	    return int(value)
    71	
    72	
    73	def _read_team(row: dict, column: str, line: int) -> str:
    74	    name = row[column].strip()
    75	
    76	    if name == "":
    77	        raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: '{column}' is blank; every club needs a name")
    78	
    79	    return name
    80	
    81	
    82	def read_matches(stream: IO[str]) -> List[Match]:
    83	    """Parse match results from an open CSV stream.
    84	
    85	    Raises `InvalidInput` -- with the line number -- on anything it cannot read.
    86	    """
    87	    reader = csv.reader(stream)
    88	
    89	    try:
    90	        raw_header = next(reader)
    91	    except StopIteration:
    92	        raise InvalidInput("the input is empty: no header row found") from None
    93	
    94	    header = _normalise_header(raw_header)
    95	    seen = Counter(header)
    96	
    97	    missing = [column for column in REQUIRED_COLUMNS if column not in header]
    98	    if missing:
    99	        raise InvalidInput(
   100	            "the header is missing required column(s): "
   101	            + ", ".join(missing)
   102	            + f". Expected at least: {', '.join(REQUIRED_COLUMNS)}"
   103	        )
   104	
   105	    # A required column appearing twice cannot be resolved: one of the two values
   106	    # would win silently, and the table would be quietly wrong. Refuse instead.
   107	    duplicated = [column for column in REQUIRED_COLUMNS if seen[column] > 1]
   108	    if duplicated:
   109	        raise InvalidInput(
   110	            "the header names these required column(s) more than once: "
   111	            + ", ".join(duplicated)
   112	        )
   113	
   114	    matches = []
   115	
   116	    for values in reader:
   117	        # csv records and physical lines are not the same thing: a quoted club
   118	        # name may span lines. `line_num` is the reader's own count of physical
   119	        # lines, which is the number a human needs in order to find the row.
   120	        line = reader.line_num
   121	
   122	        if not any(value.strip() for value in values):
   123	            continue  # a blank separator line is not an error
   124	
   125	        if len(values) != len(header):
   126	            raise InvalidInput(
   127	                f"line {line}: expected {len(header)} columns, found {len(values)}"
   128	            )
   129	
   130	        row = dict(zip(header, values))
   131	
   132	        home = _read_team(row, "home_team", line)
   133	        away = _read_team(row, "away_team", line)
   134	
   135	        if home == away:
   136	            raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: {home} cannot play itself")
   137	
   138	        matches.append(
   139	            Match(
   140	                home_team=home,
   141	                away_team=away,
   142	                home_goals=_read_goals(row, "home_goals", line),
   143	                away_goals=_read_goals(row, "away_goals", line),
   144	            )
   145	        )
   146	
   147	    return matches
   148	
   149	
   150	def write_table(standings: Iterable[Standing], stream: IO[str]) -> None:
   151	    """Write the standing table as CSV, in the conventional column order."""
   152	    writer = csv.writer(stream, lineterminator="\n")
   153	    writer.writerow(OUTPUT_COLUMNS)
   154	
   155	    for row in standings:
   156	        writer.writerow(
   157	            (
   158	                row.position,
   159	                row.team,
   160	                row.played,
   161	                row.won,
   162	                row.drawn,
   163	                row.lost,
   164	                row.goals_for,
   165	                row.goals_against,
   166	                str(row.goal_average),
   167	                row.points,
   168	            )
   169	        )

=============== src/league_table/rules.py
     1	"""The scoring and ranking rules of the English First Division, 1974/75.
     2	
     3	These are historical rules, not modern ones. See README.md for the sources.
     4	
     5	Two rules matter and both differ from the modern game:
     6	
     7	* Two points for a win, one for a draw. Three points for a win was not
     8	  introduced in England until 1981/82.
     9	* Clubs level on points are separated by goal AVERAGE (goals scored divided by
    10	  goals conceded), not goal difference. Goal difference did not replace goal
    11	  average in the Football League until 1976/77.
    12	
    13	This module is pure: no I/O, no CSV, no filesystem.
    14	"""
    15	
    16	import functools
    17	from fractions import Fraction
    18	from typing import Optional, Tuple
    19	
    20	POINTS_FOR_WIN = 2
    21	POINTS_FOR_DRAW = 1
    22	POINTS_FOR_LOSS = 0
    23	
    24	
    25	def points_for_result(goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> int:
    26	    """Points a club earns from a single match with this scoreline."""
    27	    if goals_for > goals_against:
    28	        return POINTS_FOR_WIN
    29	    if goals_for == goals_against:
    30	        return POINTS_FOR_DRAW
    31	    return POINTS_FOR_LOSS
    32	
    33	
    34	# Ranking bands for the two degenerate cases of a ratio. A club that has
    35	# conceded nothing has an unbounded average and so outranks every finite one; a
    36	# club with no goals either way (including one that has played no games) has no
    37	# average at all -- 0/0 -- and cannot outrank a club that achieved a real ratio.
    38	# Both are deliberate choices, not accidents of arithmetic; see README.md.
    39	_UNDEFINED = 0
    40	_FINITE = 1
    41	_INFINITE = 2
    42	
    43	
    44	@functools.total_ordering
    45	class GoalAverage:
    46	    """Goals scored divided by goals conceded, as a totally ordered value.
    47	
    48	    Held as a `Fraction`, never a float: averages like 14/11 and 25/19 are
    49	    compared directly against one another, and float rounding would silently
    50	    equate values that differ, mis-ordering the table.
    51	
    52	    Greater is better, so these objects sort the way the league table reads.
    53	    """
    54	
    55	    __slots__ = ("_band", "_value")
    56	
    57	    def __init__(self, goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> None:
    58	        if goals_against > 0:
    59	            self._band = _FINITE
    60	            self._value: Optional[Fraction] = Fraction(goals_for, goals_against)
    61	        elif goals_for > 0:
    62	            self._band = _INFINITE
    63	            self._value = None
    64	        else:
    65	            self._band = _UNDEFINED
    66	            self._value = None
    67	
    68	    @property
    69	    def value(self) -> Optional[Fraction]:
    70	        """The exact ratio, or None where it is infinite or undefined."""
    71	        return self._value
    72	
    73	    @property
    74	    def is_infinite(self) -> bool:
    75	        """True when the club has scored but conceded nothing."""
    76	        return self._band == _INFINITE
    77	
    78	    @property
    79	    def is_undefined(self) -> bool:
    80	        """True when the club has neither scored nor conceded (0/0)."""
    81	        return self._band == _UNDEFINED
    82	
    83	    def sort_key(self) -> Tuple[int, Fraction]:
    84	        """A plain (band, ratio) pair. Higher is better, in both elements.
    85	
    86	        Both numbers negate cleanly, so a caller wanting descending order can
    87	        simply negate them rather than wrap this in a reversing adapter.
    88	        """
    89	        ratio = Fraction(0) if self._value is None else self._value
    90	        return (self._band, ratio)
    91	
    92	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
    93	        if not isinstance(other, GoalAverage):
    94	            return NotImplemented
    95	        return self.sort_key() == other.sort_key()
    96	
    97	    def __lt__(self, other: "GoalAverage") -> bool:
    98	        if not isinstance(other, GoalAverage):
    99	            return NotImplemented
   100	        return self.sort_key() < other.sort_key()
   101	
   102	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
   103	        return hash(self.sort_key())
   104	
   105	    def __str__(self) -> str:
   106	        if self._band == _INFINITE:
   107	            return "inf"
   108	        if self._band == _UNDEFINED:
   109	            return "n/a"
   110	        return f"{float(self._value):.3f}"
   111	
   112	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
   113	        return f"GoalAverage({self})"
   114	
   115	
   116	def goal_average(goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> GoalAverage:
   117	    """The 1974/75 tie-break: goals scored divided by goals conceded.
   118	
   119	    NOT goal difference. Goal difference did not replace goal average in the
   120	    Football League until 1976/77 and is the wrong rule for this season.
   121	    """
   122	    return GoalAverage(goals_for, goals_against)
=============== src/league_table/table.py
     1	"""Turning a list of match results into a standing table.
     2	
     3	Pure domain: no I/O. The rules it applies live in `rules.py`.
     4	
     5	The tool knows nothing about weeks, matchdays or the 1974/75 season. It
     6	tabulates whatever results it is given; deciding *which* results constitute
     7	"the 10th week" is the caller's business, and is discussed in README.md.
     8	"""
     9	
    10	from typing import Dict, Iterable, List, Tuple
    11	
    12	from league_table.models import Match, Standing
    13	from league_table.rules import goal_average, points_for_result
    14	
    15	
    16	class _Tally:
    17	    """A club's running totals while the results are being read."""
    18	
    19	    __slots__ = ("won", "drawn", "lost", "goals_for", "goals_against", "points")
    20	
    21	    def __init__(self) -> None:
    22	        self.won = 0
    23	        self.drawn = 0
    24	        self.lost = 0
    25	        self.goals_for = 0
    26	        self.goals_against = 0
    27	        self.points = 0
    28	
    29	    def record(self, scored: int, conceded: int) -> None:
    30	        self.goals_for += scored
    31	        self.goals_against += conceded
    32	        self.points += points_for_result(scored, conceded)
    33	
    34	        if scored > conceded:
    35	            self.won += 1
    36	        elif scored == conceded:
    37	            self.drawn += 1
    38	        else:
    39	            self.lost += 1
    40	
    41	    @property
    42	    def played(self) -> int:
    43	        return self.won + self.drawn + self.lost
    44	
    45	
    46	def _ranking_key(entry: Tuple[str, _Tally]):
    47	    """The 1974/75 order: points, then goal average, then club name.
    48	
    49	    Points and the goal average both sort descending -- higher is better -- and
    50	    both are plain numbers, so they are simply negated. The club name breaks the
    51	    remaining ties ascending.
    52	
    53	    The name is not a historical rule. The Football League had no tie-break
    54	    beyond goal average; clubs level on both genuinely shared the position. It is
    55	    here so the output is deterministic, and it is documented as such.
    56	    """
    57	    team, tally = entry
    58	    band, ratio = goal_average(tally.goals_for, tally.goals_against).sort_key()
    59	    return (-tally.points, -band, -ratio, team)
    60	
    61	
    62	def build_table(matches: Iterable[Match]) -> List[Standing]:
    63	    """The standing table for these results, best club first."""
    64	    tallies: Dict[str, _Tally] = {}
    65	
    66	    for match in matches:
    67	        home = tallies.setdefault(match.home_team, _Tally())
    68	        away = tallies.setdefault(match.away_team, _Tally())
    69	
    70	        home.record(match.home_goals, match.away_goals)
    71	        away.record(match.away_goals, match.home_goals)
    72	
    73	    ranked = sorted(tallies.items(), key=_ranking_key)
    74	
    75	    return [
    76	        Standing(
    77	            position=position,
    78	            team=team,
    79	            played=tally.played,
    80	            won=tally.won,
    81	            drawn=tally.drawn,
    82	            lost=tally.lost,
    83	            goals_for=tally.goals_for,
    84	            goals_against=tally.goals_against,
    85	            goal_average=goal_average(tally.goals_for, tally.goals_against),
    86	            points=tally.points,
    87	        )
    88	        for position, (team, tally) in enumerate(ranked, start=1)
    89	    ]

     1	"""The command line. Argument parsing and streams; no rules live here.
     2	
     3	    league-table                       # stdin  -> stdout
     4	    league-table results.csv           # file   -> stdout
     5	    league-table results.csv -o t.csv  # file   -> file
     6	    cat results.csv | league-table     # a plain unix filter
     7	
     8	The table is computed in full before a single byte is written, so a malformed
     9	input never leaves a half-written table behind.
    10	"""
    11	
    12	import argparse
    13	import contextlib
    14	import io
    15	import os
    16	import sys
    17	from collections import Counter
    18	from typing import IO, Iterator, List, Optional, Sequence
    19	
    20	from league_table import __version__
    21	from league_table.csvio import InvalidInput, read_matches, write_table
    22	from league_table.models import Match, Standing
    23	from league_table.table import build_table
    24	
    25	EXIT_OK = 0

    28	# what a shell would have reported had we not caught the error.

    30	
    31	_STDIO = "-"
    32	
    33	_PRETTY_COLUMNS = ("Pos", "Team", "P", "W", "D", "L", "F", "A", "GAvg", "Pts")
    34	
    35	
    36	def _parser() -> argparse.ArgumentParser:
    37	    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
    38	        prog="league-table",
    39	        description=(
    40	            "Calculate a football league standing table from match results, "
    41	            "using the rules of the English First Division in 1974/75: two points "
    42	            "for a win, one for a draw, and clubs level on points separated by "
    43	            "goal average (goals scored divided by goals conceded)."
    44	        ),
    45	    )
    46	    parser.add_argument(
    47	        "input",
    48	        nargs="?",
    49	        default=_STDIO,
    50	        help="CSV of results; omit or pass '-' to read standard input",
    51	    )
    52	    parser.add_argument(
    53	        "-o",
    54	        "--output",
    55	        default=_STDIO,
    56	        help="where to write the table; omit or pass '-' for standard output",
    57	    )
    58	    parser.add_argument(
    59	        "--pretty",
    60	        action="store_true",
    61	        help="print an aligned text table instead of CSV",
    62	    )
    63	    parser.add_argument(
    64	        "--version",
    65	        action="version",
    66	        version=f"league-table {__version__}",
    67	    )
    68	    return parser
    69	
    70	
    71	def _warn_about_repeated_fixtures(matches: Sequence[Match], stderr: IO[str]) -> None:
    72	    """Flag any pairing that appears more than once at the same ground.
    73	
    74	    Not an error: some leagues really do play the same fixture twice at the same
    75	    ground in a season, and this tool tabulates whatever league it is handed. But
    76	    a duplicated row in a results feed is the commonest way to end up with a
    77	    confidently wrong table, so it does not go through in silence.
    78	    """
    79	    played = Counter((match.home_team, match.away_team) for match in matches)
    80	
    81	    for (home, away), count in played.items():
    82	        if count > 1:
    83	            times = "twice" if count == 2 else f"{count} times"
    84	            print(
    85	                f"league-table: warning: {home} v {away} appears {times}; "
    86	                "every occurrence has been counted",
    87	                file=stderr,
    88	            )
    89	
    90	
    91	@contextlib.contextmanager
    92	def _opened(path: str, mode: str, stream: IO[str]) -> Iterator[IO[str]]:
    93	    """Yield the named file, or the given standard stream for '-'.
    94	
    95	    Input is decoded as utf-8-sig, which strips a byte order mark if one is
    96	    present and is a plain utf-8 read if not. Excel and Google Sheets both write
    97	    a BOM by default, so without this the commonest real-world CSV in existence
    98	    fails with a baffling "missing required column: home_team".
    99	    """
   100	    if path == _STDIO:
   101	        yield stream
   102	        return
   103	
   104	    encoding = "utf-8-sig" if mode == "r" else "utf-8"
   105	
   106	    try:
   107	        handle = open(path, mode, newline="", encoding=encoding)
   108	    except OSError as error:
   109	        raise InvalidInput(f"cannot open {path}: {error.strerror}") from None
   110	
   111	    try:
   112	        # A decode error is bad input, not a crash: UnicodeError is a ValueError,
   113	        # not an OSError, so it would otherwise sail past as a traceback.
   114	        yield handle
   115	    except UnicodeError as error:
   116	        raise InvalidInput(f"cannot read {path}: {error}") from None
   117	    finally:
   118	        handle.close()
   119	
   120	
   121	def _silence_stdout(stdout: IO[str]) -> None:
   122	    """Redirect the real stdout to /dev/null, if there is a real one.
   123	
   124	    Only meaningful for an actual file descriptor; under test the stream is an
   125	    in-memory buffer with nothing to redirect, so there is nothing to do.
   126	    """
   127	    try:
   128	        fileno = stdout.fileno()
   129	    except (AttributeError, io.UnsupportedOperation):
   130	        return
   131	
   132	    devnull = os.open(os.devnull, os.O_WRONLY)
   133	    os.dup2(devnull, fileno)
   134	
   135	
   136	def _write_pretty(standings: Sequence[Standing], stream: IO[str]) -> None:
   137	    rows = [
   138	        (
   139	            str(row.position),
   140	            row.team,
   141	            str(row.played),
   142	            str(row.won),
   143	            str(row.drawn),
   144	            str(row.lost),
   145	            str(row.goals_for),
   146	            str(row.goals_against),
   147	            str(row.goal_average),
   148	            str(row.points),
   149	        )
   150	        for row in standings
   151	    ]
   152	
   153	    widths = [
   154	        max(len(cell) for cell in column) for column in zip(_PRETTY_COLUMNS, *rows)
   155	    ]
   156	
   157	    def line(cells: Sequence[str]) -> str:
   158	        # The club name reads best left-aligned; the numbers line up right.
   159	        rendered = [
   160	            cell.ljust(width) if index == 1 else cell.rjust(width)
   161	            for index, (cell, width) in enumerate(zip(cells, widths))
   162	        ]
   163	        return "  ".join(rendered).rstrip()
   164	
   165	    stream.write(line(_PRETTY_COLUMNS) + "\n")
   166	    for row in rows:
   167	        stream.write(line(row) + "\n")
   168	
   169	
   170	def main(
   171	    argv: Optional[List[str]] = None,
   172	    stdin: Optional[IO[str]] = None,
   173	    stdout: Optional[IO[str]] = None,
   174	    stderr: Optional[IO[str]] = None,
   175	) -> int:
   176	    """Run the tool. Returns the process exit code.
   177	
   178	    The streams are injectable so the tests can drive the real entry point
   179	    without spawning a process or monkeypatching `sys`.
   180	    """
   181	    stdin = sys.stdin if stdin is None else stdin
   182	    stdout = sys.stdout if stdout is None else stdout
   183	    stderr = sys.stderr if stderr is None else stderr
   184	
   185	    args = _parser().parse_args(argv)
   186	
   187	    try:
   188	        with _opened(args.input, "r", stdin) as source:
   189	            matches = read_matches(source)
   190	
   191	        _warn_about_repeated_fixtures(matches, stderr)
   192	
   193	        standings = build_table(matches)
   194	
   195	        # Render to memory first: if anything above had failed we would not have
   196	        # touched the output at all, and this keeps that guarantee for the write.
   197	        rendered = io.StringIO()
   198	        if args.pretty:
   199	            _write_pretty(standings, rendered)
   200	        else:
   201	            write_table(standings, rendered)
   202	
   203	        with _opened(args.output, "w", stdout) as destination:
   204	            destination.write(rendered.getvalue())
   205	            # Flush while we can still do something about a failure. Left to the
   206	            # interpreter's exit-time flush, a broken pipe or a full disk would
   207	            # surface as a traceback and an exit code we never chose.
   208	            destination.flush()
   209	
   210	    except InvalidInput as error:
   211	        print(f"league-table: {error}", file=stderr)
   212	        return EXIT_INVALID_INPUT
   213	
   214	    except BrokenPipeError:
   215	        # `league-table results.csv | head -1` closes the pipe under us. That is
   216	        # a reader losing interest, not an error -- but Python flushes stdout
   217	        # again on the way out, which would raise a second BrokenPipeError and
   218	        # print a traceback. Point the fd at /dev/null so that final flush has
   219	        # somewhere harmless to go, and exit the way a unix filter should.
   220	        _silence_stdout(stdout)
   221	        return EXIT_BROKEN_PIPE
   222	
   223	    return EXIT_OK
=============== src/league_table/csvio.py
     1	"""CSV in, CSV out. All the I/O lives here; none of the rules do.
     2	
     3	The parser needs four columns -- home_team, away_team, home_goals, away_goals --
     4	and ignores every other column it finds. That is what lets the data files carry
     5	`round` and `date` for a human's benefit while the tool stays entirely ignorant
     6	of matchdays and seasons.
     7	"""
     8	
     9	import csv
    10	from collections import Counter
    11	from typing import IO, Iterable, List, Sequence
    12	
    13	from league_table.models import Match, Standing
    14	
    15	REQUIRED_COLUMNS = ("home_team", "away_team", "home_goals", "away_goals")
    16	
    17	OUTPUT_COLUMNS = (
    18	    "Position",
    19	    "Team",
    20	    "Played",
    21	    "Won",
    22	    "Drawn",
    23	    "Lost",
    24	    "GoalsFor",
    25	    "GoalsAgainst",
    26	    "GoalAverage",
    27	    "Points",
    28	)
    29	
    30	
    31	class InvalidInput(Exception):
    32	    """The input is not a readable set of results.
    33	
    34	    Carries a message naming the offending line, so a human can go and fix it.
    35	    """
    36	
    37	
    38	def _normalise_header(fieldnames: Sequence[str]) -> List[str]:
    39	    # A leading BOM would otherwise ride along on the first column name, so that
    40	    # 'home_team' fails to match 'home_team'. Files opened by this module are
    41	    # decoded as utf-8-sig, but a caller can hand us any stream -- stdin, most
    42	    # obviously -- so strip it here too.
    43	    return [(name or "").lstrip("﻿").strip().lower() for name in fieldnames]
    44	
    45	
    46	def _is_goal_count(value: str) -> bool:
    47	    """True for a plain run of ASCII digits, and nothing else."""
    48	    return value.isascii() and value.isdigit()
    49	
    50	
    51	def _read_goals(row: dict, column: str, line: int) -> int:
    52	    # Every required column is guaranteed present: the header is checked once up
    53	    # front, and rows of the wrong width are rejected before we get here.
    54	    value = row[column].strip()
    55	
    56	    if value == "":
    57	        raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: missing a value for '{column}'")
    58	
    59	    if value.startswith("-") and _is_goal_count(value[1:]):
    60	        raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: '{column}' cannot be negative, got {value}")
    61	
    62	    # Deliberately stricter than int(), which accepts PEP 515 underscores
    63	    # ("1_0" -> 10) and non-ASCII decimal digits. Neither is a goal count, and
    64	    # both would silently produce a wrong table rather than an error.
    65	    if not _is_goal_count(value):
    66	        raise InvalidInput(
    67	            f"line {line}: '{column}' must be a whole number of goals, got {value!r}"
    68	        )
    69	
    70	    return int(value)
    71	
    72	
    73	def _read_team(row: dict, column: str, line: int) -> str:
    74	    name = row[column].strip()
    75	
    76	    if name == "":
    77	        raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: '{column}' is blank; every club needs a name")
    78	
    79	    return name
    80	
    81	
    82	def read_matches(stream: IO[str]) -> List[Match]:
    83	    """Parse match results from an open CSV stream.
    84	
    85	    Raises `InvalidInput` -- with the line number -- on anything it cannot read.
    86	    """
    87	    reader = csv.reader(stream)
    88	
    89	    try:
    90	        raw_header = next(reader)
    91	    except StopIteration:
    92	        raise InvalidInput("the input is empty: no header row found") from None
    93	
    94	    header = _normalise_header(raw_header)
    95	    seen = Counter(header)
    96	
    97	    missing = [column for column in REQUIRED_COLUMNS if column not in header]
    98	    if missing:
    99	        raise InvalidInput(
   100	            "the header is missing required column(s): "
   101	            + ", ".join(missing)
   102	            + f". Expected at least: {', '.join(REQUIRED_COLUMNS)}"
   103	        )
   104	
   105	    # A required column appearing twice cannot be resolved: one of the two values
   106	    # would win silently, and the table would be quietly wrong. Refuse instead.
   107	    duplicated = [column for column in REQUIRED_COLUMNS if seen[column] > 1]
   108	    if duplicated:
   109	        raise InvalidInput(
   110	            "the header names these required column(s) more than once: "
   111	            + ", ".join(duplicated)
   112	        )
   113	
   114	    matches = []
   115	
   116	    for values in reader:
   117	        # csv records and physical lines are not the same thing: a quoted club
   118	        # name may span lines. `line_num` is the reader's own count of physical
   119	        # lines, which is the number a human needs in order to find the row.
   120	        line = reader.line_num
   121	
   122	        if not any(value.strip() for value in values):
   123	            continue  # a blank separator line is not an error
   124	
   125	        if len(values) != len(header):
   126	            raise InvalidInput(
   127	                f"line {line}: expected {len(header)} columns, found {len(values)}"
   128	            )
   129	
   130	        row = dict(zip(header, values))
   131	
   132	        home = _read_team(row, "home_team", line)
   133	        away = _read_team(row, "away_team", line)
   134	
   135	        if home == away:
   136	            raise InvalidInput(f"line {line}: {home} cannot play itself")
   137	
   138	        matches.append(
   139	            Match(
   140	                home_team=home,
   141	                away_team=away,
   142	                home_goals=_read_goals(row, "home_goals", line),
   143	                away_goals=_read_goals(row, "away_goals", line),
   144	            )
   145	        )
   146	
   147	    return matches
   148	
   149	
   150	def write_table(standings: Iterable[Standing], stream: IO[str]) -> None:
   151	    """Write the standing table as CSV, in the conventional column order."""
   152	    writer = csv.writer(stream, lineterminator="\n")
   153	    writer.writerow(OUTPUT_COLUMNS)
   154	
   155	    for row in standings:
   156	        writer.writerow(
   157	            (
   158	                row.position,
   159	                row.team,
   160	                row.played,
   161	                row.won,
   162	                row.drawn,
   163	                row.lost,
   164	                row.goals_for,
   165	                row.goals_against,
   166	                str(row.goal_average),
   167	                row.points,
   168	            )
   169	        )

     1	"""The values the domain works in: a match result, and a row of the table."""
     2	
     3	from dataclasses import dataclass
     4	
     5	from league_table.rules import GoalAverage
     6	
     7	
     8	@dataclass(frozen=True)
     9	class Match:
    10	    """One played match. A result, not a fixture: the score is known."""
    11	
    12	    home_team: str
    13	    away_team: str
    14	    home_goals: int
    15	    away_goals: int

    17	
    18	@dataclass(frozen=True)
    19	class Standing:
    20	    """One club's row in the standing table.
    21	
    22	    There is deliberately no goal-difference column. The 1974/75 table was
    23	    published with goals for, goals against and goal average, and showing a
    24	    difference alongside them would invite exactly the confusion this exercise
    25	    is about.
    26	    """
    27	
    28	    position: int
    29	    team: str
    30	    played: int
    31	    won: int
    32	    drawn: int
    33	    lost: int
    34	    goals_for: int
    35	    goals_against: int
    36	    goal_average: GoalAverage
    37	    points: int
=============== src/league_table/rules.py
     1	"""The scoring and ranking rules of the English First Division, 1974/75.
     2	
     3	These are historical rules, not modern ones. See README.md for the sources.
     4	
     5	Two rules matter and both differ from the modern game:
     6	
     7	* Two points for a win, one for a draw. Three points for a win was not
     8	  introduced in England until 1981/82.
     9	* Clubs level on points are separated by goal AVERAGE (goals scored divided by
    10	  goals conceded), not goal difference. Goal difference did not replace goal
    11	  average in the Football League until 1976/77.
    12	
    13	This module is pure: no I/O, no CSV, no filesystem.
    14	"""
    15	
    16	import functools
    17	from fractions import Fraction
    18	from typing import Optional, Tuple
    19	
    20	POINTS_FOR_WIN = 2
    21	POINTS_FOR_DRAW = 1
    22	POINTS_FOR_LOSS = 0
    23	
    24	
    25	def points_for_result(goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> int:
    26	    """Points a club earns from a single match with this scoreline."""
    27	    if goals_for > goals_against:
    28	        return POINTS_FOR_WIN
    29	    if goals_for == goals_against:
    30	        return POINTS_FOR_DRAW
    31	    return POINTS_FOR_LOSS
    32	
    33	
    34	# Ranking bands for the two degenerate cases of a ratio. A club that has
    35	# conceded nothing has an unbounded average and so outranks every finite one; a
    36	# club with no goals either way (including one that has played no games) has no
    37	# average at all -- 0/0 -- and cannot outrank a club that achieved a real ratio.
    38	# Both are deliberate choices, not accidents of arithmetic; see README.md.
    39	_UNDEFINED = 0
    40	_FINITE = 1
    41	_INFINITE = 2
    42	
    43	
    44	@functools.total_ordering
    45	class GoalAverage:
    46	    """Goals scored divided by goals conceded, as a totally ordered value.
    47	
    48	    Held as a `Fraction`, never a float: averages like 14/11 and 25/19 are
    49	    compared directly against one another, and float rounding would silently
    50	    equate values that differ, mis-ordering the table.
    51	
    52	    Greater is better, so these objects sort the way the league table reads.
    53	    """
    54	
    55	    __slots__ = ("_band", "_value")
    56	
    57	    def __init__(self, goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> None:
    58	        if goals_against > 0:
    59	            self._band = _FINITE
    60	            self._value: Optional[Fraction] = Fraction(goals_for, goals_against)
    61	        elif goals_for > 0:
    62	            self._band = _INFINITE
    63	            self._value = None
    64	        else:
    65	            self._band = _UNDEFINED
    66	            self._value = None
    67	
    68	    @property
    69	    def value(self) -> Optional[Fraction]:
    70	        """The exact ratio, or None where it is infinite or undefined."""
    71	        return self._value
    72	
    73	    @property
    74	    def is_infinite(self) -> bool:
    75	        """True when the club has scored but conceded nothing."""
    76	        return self._band == _INFINITE
    77	
    78	    @property
    79	    def is_undefined(self) -> bool:
    80	        """True when the club has neither scored nor conceded (0/0)."""
    81	        return self._band == _UNDEFINED
    82	
    83	    def sort_key(self) -> Tuple[int, Fraction]:
    84	        """A plain (band, ratio) pair. Higher is better, in both elements.
    85	
    86	        Both numbers negate cleanly, so a caller wanting descending order can
    87	        simply negate them rather than wrap this in a reversing adapter.
    88	        """
    89	        ratio = Fraction(0) if self._value is None else self._value
    90	        return (self._band, ratio)
    91	
    92	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
    93	        if not isinstance(other, GoalAverage):
    94	            return NotImplemented
    95	        return self.sort_key() == other.sort_key()
    96	
    97	    def __lt__(self, other: "GoalAverage") -> bool:
    98	        if not isinstance(other, GoalAverage):
    99	            return NotImplemented
   100	        return self.sort_key() < other.sort_key()
   101	
   102	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
   103	        return hash(self.sort_key())
   104	
   105	    def __str__(self) -> str:
   106	        if self._band == _INFINITE:
   107	            return "inf"
   108	        if self._band == _UNDEFINED:
   109	            return "n/a"
   110	        return f"{float(self._value):.3f}"
   111	
   112	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
   113	        return f"GoalAverage({self})"
   114	
   115	
   116	def goal_average(goals_for: int, goals_against: int) -> GoalAverage:
   117	    """The 1974/75 tie-break: goals scored divided by goals conceded.
   118	
   119	    NOT goal difference. Goal difference did not replace goal average in the
   120	    Football League until 1976/77 and is the wrong rule for this season.
   121	    """
   122	    return GoalAverage(goals_for, goals_against)
=============== src/league_table/table.py
     1	"""Turning a list of match results into a standing table.
     2	
     3	Pure domain: no I/O. The rules it applies live in `rules.py`.
     4	
     5	The tool knows nothing about weeks, matchdays or the 1974/75 season. It
     6	tabulates whatever results it is given; deciding *which* results constitute
     7	"the 10th week" is the caller's business, and is discussed in README.md.
     8	"""
     9	
    10	from typing import Dict, Iterable, List, Tuple
    11	
    12	from league_table.models import Match, Standing
    13	from league_table.rules import goal_average, points_for_result
    14	
    15	
    16	class _Tally:
    17	    """A club's running totals while the results are being read."""
    18	
    19	    __slots__ = ("won", "drawn", "lost", "goals_for", "goals_against", "points")
    20	
    21	    def __init__(self) -> None:
    22	        self.won = 0
    23	        self.drawn = 0
    24	        self.lost = 0
    25	        self.goals_for = 0
    26	        self.goals_against = 0
    27	        self.points = 0
    28	
    29	    def record(self, scored: int, conceded: int) -> None:
    30	        self.goals_for += scored
    31	        self.goals_against += conceded
    32	        self.points += points_for_result(scored, conceded)
    33	
    34	        if scored > conceded:
    35	            self.won += 1
    36	        elif scored == conceded:
    37	            self.drawn += 1
    38	        else:
    39	            self.lost += 1
    40	
    41	    @property
    42	    def played(self) -> int:
    43	        return self.won + self.drawn + self.lost
    44	
    45	
    46	def _ranking_key(entry: Tuple[str, _Tally]):
    47	    """The 1974/75 order: points, then goal average, then club name.
    48	
    49	    Points and the goal average both sort descending -- higher is better -- and
    50	    both are plain numbers, so they are simply negated. The club name breaks the
    51	    remaining ties ascending.
    52	
    53	    The name is not a historical rule. The Football League had no tie-break
    54	    beyond goal average; clubs level on both genuinely shared the position. It is
    55	    here so the output is deterministic, and it is documented as such.
    56	    """
    57	    team, tally = entry
    58	    band, ratio = goal_average(tally.goals_for, tally.goals_against).sort_key()
    59	    return (-tally.points, -band, -ratio, team)
    60	
    61	
    62	def build_table(matches: Iterable[Match]) -> List[Standing]:
    63	    """The standing table for these results, best club first."""
    64	    tallies: Dict[str, _Tally] = {}
    65	
    66	    for match in matches:
    67	        home = tallies.setdefault(match.home_team, _Tally())
    68	        away = tallies.setdefault(match.away_team, _Tally())
    69	
    70	        home.record(match.home_goals, match.away_goals)
    71	        away.record(match.away_goals, match.home_goals)
    72	
    73	    ranked = sorted(tallies.items(), key=_ranking_key)
    74	
    75	    return [
    76	        Standing(
    77	            position=position,
    78	            team=team,
    79	            played=tally.played,
    80	            won=tally.won,
    81	            drawn=tally.drawn,
    82	            lost=tally.lost,
    83	            goals_for=tally.goals_for,
    84	            goals_against=tally.goals_against,
    85	            goal_average=goal_average(tally.goals_for, tally.goals_against),
    86	            points=tally.points,
    87	        )
    88	        for position, (team, tally) in enumerate(ranked, start=1)
    89	    ]

content.content:
1	"""The actual answer to the exercise, pinned down.
     2	
     3	These tests run the real 1974/75 results through the real CLI and assert on the
     4	real table. They are the regression lock: if anyone changes the rules, the
     5	parsing or the sort, the English First Division of 1974/75 changes shape and
     6	these fail.
     7	"""
     8	
     9	import csv
    10	import io
    11	from pathlib import Path
    12	
    13	from league_table.cli import EXIT_OK, main
    14	
    15	DATA = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "data"
    16	
    17	ROUNDS_1_TO_10 = DATA / "results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv"
    18	PLAYED_BY_28_SEP = DATA / "results_1974-75_played_by_1974-09-28.csv"
    19	
    20	STANDINGS_AFTER_MATCHDAY_10 = DATA / "standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv"
    21	STANDINGS_AS_OF_28_SEP = DATA / "standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv"
    22	
    23	
    24	def table_for(results: Path):
    25	    """Run the CLI over a results file and return the parsed table rows."""
    26	    out = io.StringIO()
    27	    code = main([str(results)], stdout=out)
    28	
    29	    assert code == EXIT_OK
    30	    return list(csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(out.getvalue())))
    31	
    32	
    33	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    34	# The submitted answer: the table after ten matchdays.
    35	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    36	
    37	
    38	def test_the_input_holds_every_match_of_the_first_ten_rounds():
    39	    rows = list(csv.DictReader(ROUNDS_1_TO_10.open()))
    40	
    41	    # 22 clubs, 11 matches a round, 10 rounds.
    42	    assert len(rows) == 110
    43	    assert {row["round"] for row in rows} == {str(n) for n in range(1, 11)}
    44	
    45	
    46	def test_every_club_has_played_exactly_ten_matches():
    47	    table = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)
    48	
    49	    assert len(table) == 22
    50	    assert {row["Played"] for row in table} == {"10"}
    51	
    52	
    53	def test_ipswich_town_lead_the_first_division_after_ten_matchdays():
    54	    top = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)[0]
    55	
    56	    assert top["Team"] == "Ipswich Town"
    57	    assert top["Points"] == "16"
    58	    assert (top["Won"], top["Drawn"], top["Lost"]) == ("8", "0", "2")
    59	    assert (top["GoalsFor"], top["GoalsAgainst"]) == ("18", "6")
    60	    assert top["GoalAverage"] == "3.000"
    61	
    62	
    63	def test_the_full_top_ten_after_matchday_ten():
    64	    table = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)
    65	
    66	    assert [(row["Team"], row["Points"]) for row in table[:10]] == [
    67	        ("Ipswich Town", "16"),
    68	        ("Newcastle United", "14"),
    69	        ("Manchester City", "14"),
    70	        ("Middlesbrough", "13"),
    71	        ("Liverpool", "13"),
    72	        ("Everton", "13"),
    73	        ("Sheffield United", "13"),
    74	        ("Derby County", "11"),
    75	        ("Stoke City", "11"),
    76	        ("Wolverhampton Wanderers", "11"),
    77	    ]
    78	
    79	
    80	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    81	# The historical rule, visible in the real season.
    82	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    83	
    84	
    85	def test_goal_average_reorders_the_real_1974_75_table_against_goal_difference():
    86	    """The rule is not academic: it moves clubs in the actual table.
    87	
    88	    Middlesbrough and Liverpool both finish matchday 10 on 13 points.
    89	
    90	        Middlesbrough  15 scored,  7 conceded -> average 2.143, difference +8
    91	        Liverpool      17 scored,  8 conceded -> average 2.125, difference +9
    92	
    93	    Goal average puts Middlesbrough 4th and Liverpool 5th. Goal difference --
    94	    the modern rule, and the wrong one for 1974/75 -- would swap them. This is
    95	    the exercise's whole point, occurring in the submitted answer itself.
    96	    """
    97	    table = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)
    98	    by_team = {row["Team"]: row for row in table}
    99	
   100	    boro, pool = by_team["Middlesbrough"], by_team["Liverpool"]
   101	
   102	    assert boro["Points"] == pool["Points"] == "13"
   103	    assert int(boro["Position"]) < int(pool["Position"])  # goal average: Boro ahead
   104	
   105	    # And goal difference would have said the opposite.
   106	    boro_difference = int(boro["GoalsFor"]) - int(boro["GoalsAgainst"])
   107	    pool_difference = int(pool["GoalsFor"]) - int(pool["GoalsAgainst"])
   108	    assert pool_difference > boro_difference
   109	
   110	
   111	def test_goal_average_separates_three_clubs_that_goal_difference_would_tie():
   112	    """The bottom three all end matchday 10 on 6 points AND -5 goal difference.
   113	
   114	        Tottenham Hotspur    12-17  -> difference -5, average 0.706
   115	        Arsenal              10-15  -> difference -5, average 0.667
   116	        Queens Park Rangers   8-13  -> difference -5, average 0.615
   117	
   118	    Goal difference cannot separate them at all. Goal average orders them
   119	    cleanly, and that order is the one the 1974/75 table shows.
   120	    """
   121	    table = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)
   122	    bottom_three = table[-3:]
   123	
   124	    assert [row["Team"] for row in bottom_three] == [
   125	        "Tottenham Hotspur",
   126	        "Arsenal",
   127	        "Queens Park Rangers",
   128	    ]
   129	    assert {row["Points"] for row in bottom_three} == {"6"}
   130	    assert {
   131	        int(row["GoalsFor"]) - int(row["GoalsAgainst"]) for row in bottom_three
   132	    } == {-5}  # identical on the modern rule
   133	    assert [row["GoalAverage"] for row in bottom_three] == ["0.706", "0.667", "0.615"]
   134	
   135	
   136	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   137	# The alternative reading, also shipped: the table as it actually stood.
   138	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   139	
   140	
   141	def test_the_28_september_table_reflects_the_three_postponed_fixtures():
   142	    """Three round-9 games were postponed and played in Dec 1974 / Apr 1975.
   143	
   144	    So on the evening of matchday 10, six clubs had played only nine games. This
   145	    is the table as it truly stood, and the reason the submitted answer uses the
   146	    matchday reading instead. See README.md.
   147	    """
   148	    table = table_for(PLAYED_BY_28_SEP)
   149	    played_nine = {row["Team"] for row in table if row["Played"] == "9"}
   150	
   151	    assert played_nine == {
   152	        "Newcastle United",
   153	        "Middlesbrough",
   154	        "Leicester City",
   155	        "Leeds United",
   156	        "Arsenal",
   157	        "Tottenham Hotspur",
   158	    }
   159	    assert table[0]["Team"] == "Ipswich Town"  # top either way
   160	
   161	
   162	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   163	# The committed output files must match what the tool actually produces.
   164	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   165	
   166	
   167	def test_the_committed_output_files_are_what_the_tool_produces_today():
   168	    for results, expected in (
   169	        (ROUNDS_1_TO_10, STANDINGS_AFTER_MATCHDAY_10),
   170	        (PLAYED_BY_28_SEP, STANDINGS_AS_OF_28_SEP),
   171	    ):
   172	        produced = io.StringIO()
   173	
   174	        assert main([str(results)], stdout=produced) == EXIT_OK
   175	        assert produced.getvalue() == expected.read_text(), f"{expected.name} is stale"
   176	
   177	
   178	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   179	# The calendar reading, shipped so the ambiguity can be judged rather than
   180	# taken on trust -- and, as it happens, the strongest validation in the repo.
   181	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   182	
   183	PLAYED_BY_19_OCT = DATA / "results_1974-75_played_by_1974-10-19.csv"
   184	STANDINGS_AS_OF_19_OCT = DATA / "standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv"
   185	
   186	
   187	def test_the_tenth_calendar_week_table_matches_11v11s_published_table_exactly():
   188	    """Our table for 19 October 1974, against the one 11v11 publishes.
   189	
   190	    This is the closest thing to a ground truth available: a historical table,
   191	    published by an archive, that prints its own goal-average column and orders
   192	    the clubs by it. Every row below was read from
   193	    https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/19-october-1974/
   194	
   195	    It exercises everything at once -- the match data, the two-points rule, the
   196	    goal-average ratio, the ordering, and the handling of clubs on unequal games
   197	    played (six postponed fixtures leave Leicester on 12 and ten clubs on 13).
   198	
   199	    If any of that were wrong, this test could not pass.
   200	    """
   201	    published = [
   202	        # team, P, W, D, L, GF, GA, GAvg, Pts
   203	        ("Liverpool", "13", "9", "1", "3", "21", "8", "2.625", "19"),
   204	        ("Manchester City", "14", "8", "3", "3", "19", "15", "1.267", "19"),
   205	        ("Ipswich Town", "14", "8", "1", "5", "18", "9", "2.000", "17"),
   206	        ("Everton", "14", "4", "9", "1", "19", "16", "1.188", "17"),
   207	        ("Middlesbrough", "13", "6", "4", "3", "19", "14", "1.357", "16"),
   208	        ("Stoke City", "13", "6", "4", "3", "20", "15", "1.333", "16"),
   209	        ("Derby County", "14", "5", "6", "3", "21", "19", "1.105", "16"),
   210	        ("Burnley", "14", "7", "1", "6", "23", "23", "1.000", "15"),
   211	        ("Newcastle United", "13", "5", "5", "3", "19", "19", "1.000", "15"),
   212	        ("West Ham United", "14", "5", "4", "5", "25", "22", "1.136", "14"),
   213	        ("Birmingham City", "14", "6", "2", "6", "20", "20", "1.000", "14"),
   214	        ("Wolverhampton Wanderers", "14", "4", "6", "4", "15", "15", "1.000", "14"),
   215	        ("Sheffield United", "14", "5", "4", "5", "19", "24", "0.792", "14"),
   216	        ("Carlisle United", "14", "5", "3", "6", "12", "12", "1.000", "13"),

toolUseResult.stdout:
1	"""The actual answer to the exercise, pinned down.
     2	
     3	These tests run the real 1974/75 results through the real CLI and assert on the
     4	real table. They are the regression lock: if anyone changes the rules, the
     5	parsing or the sort, the English First Division of 1974/75 changes shape and
     6	these fail.
     7	"""
     8	
     9	import csv
    10	import io
    11	from pathlib import Path
    12	
    13	from league_table.cli import EXIT_OK, main
    14	
    15	DATA = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "data"
    16	
    17	ROUNDS_1_TO_10 = DATA / "results_1974-75_rounds_1-10.csv"
    18	PLAYED_BY_28_SEP = DATA / "results_1974-75_played_by_1974-09-28.csv"
    19	
    20	STANDINGS_AFTER_MATCHDAY_10 = DATA / "standings_1974-75_after_matchday_10.csv"
    21	STANDINGS_AS_OF_28_SEP = DATA / "standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-09-28.csv"
    22	
    23	
    24	def table_for(results: Path):
    25	    """Run the CLI over a results file and return the parsed table rows."""
    26	    out = io.StringIO()
    27	    code = main([str(results)], stdout=out)
    28	
    29	    assert code == EXIT_OK
    30	    return list(csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(out.getvalue())))
    31	
    32	
    33	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    34	# The submitted answer: the table after ten matchdays.
    35	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    36	
    37	
    38	def test_the_input_holds_every_match_of_the_first_ten_rounds():
    39	    rows = list(csv.DictReader(ROUNDS_1_TO_10.open()))
    40	
    41	    # 22 clubs, 11 matches a round, 10 rounds.
    42	    assert len(rows) == 110
    43	    assert {row["round"] for row in rows} == {str(n) for n in range(1, 11)}
    44	
    45	
    46	def test_every_club_has_played_exactly_ten_matches():
    47	    table = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)
    48	
    49	    assert len(table) == 22
    50	    assert {row["Played"] for row in table} == {"10"}
    51	
    52	
    53	def test_ipswich_town_lead_the_first_division_after_ten_matchdays():
    54	    top = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)[0]
    55	
    56	    assert top["Team"] == "Ipswich Town"
    57	    assert top["Points"] == "16"
    58	    assert (top["Won"], top["Drawn"], top["Lost"]) == ("8", "0", "2")
    59	    assert (top["GoalsFor"], top["GoalsAgainst"]) == ("18", "6")
    60	    assert top["GoalAverage"] == "3.000"
    61	
    62	
    63	def test_the_full_top_ten_after_matchday_ten():
    64	    table = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)
    65	
    66	    assert [(row["Team"], row["Points"]) for row in table[:10]] == [
    67	        ("Ipswich Town", "16"),
    68	        ("Newcastle United", "14"),
    69	        ("Manchester City", "14"),
    70	        ("Middlesbrough", "13"),
    71	        ("Liverpool", "13"),
    72	        ("Everton", "13"),
    73	        ("Sheffield United", "13"),
    74	        ("Derby County", "11"),
    75	        ("Stoke City", "11"),
    76	        ("Wolverhampton Wanderers", "11"),
    77	    ]
    78	
    79	
    80	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    81	# The historical rule, visible in the real season.
    82	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    83	
    84	
    85	def test_goal_average_reorders_the_real_1974_75_table_against_goal_difference():
    86	    """The rule is not academic: it moves clubs in the actual table.
    87	
    88	    Middlesbrough and Liverpool both finish matchday 10 on 13 points.
    89	
    90	        Middlesbrough  15 scored,  7 conceded -> average 2.143, difference +8
    91	        Liverpool      17 scored,  8 conceded -> average 2.125, difference +9
    92	
    93	    Goal average puts Middlesbrough 4th and Liverpool 5th. Goal difference --
    94	    the modern rule, and the wrong one for 1974/75 -- would swap them. This is
    95	    the exercise's whole point, occurring in the submitted answer itself.
    96	    """
    97	    table = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)
    98	    by_team = {row["Team"]: row for row in table}
    99	
   100	    boro, pool = by_team["Middlesbrough"], by_team["Liverpool"]
   101	
   102	    assert boro["Points"] == pool["Points"] == "13"
   103	    assert int(boro["Position"]) < int(pool["Position"])  # goal average: Boro ahead
   104	
   105	    # And goal difference would have said the opposite.
   106	    boro_difference = int(boro["GoalsFor"]) - int(boro["GoalsAgainst"])
   107	    pool_difference = int(pool["GoalsFor"]) - int(pool["GoalsAgainst"])
   108	    assert pool_difference > boro_difference
   109	
   110	
   111	def test_goal_average_separates_three_clubs_that_goal_difference_would_tie():
   112	    """The bottom three all end matchday 10 on 6 points AND -5 goal difference.
   113	
   114	        Tottenham Hotspur    12-17  -> difference -5, average 0.706
   115	        Arsenal              10-15  -> difference -5, average 0.667
   116	        Queens Park Rangers   8-13  -> difference -5, average 0.615
   117	
   118	    Goal difference cannot separate them at all. Goal average orders them
   119	    cleanly, and that order is the one the 1974/75 table shows.
   120	    """
   121	    table = table_for(ROUNDS_1_TO_10)
   122	    bottom_three = table[-3:]
   123	
   124	    assert [row["Team"] for row in bottom_three] == [
   125	        "Tottenham Hotspur",
   126	        "Arsenal",
   127	        "Queens Park Rangers",
   128	    ]
   129	    assert {row["Points"] for row in bottom_three} == {"6"}
   130	    assert {
   131	        int(row["GoalsFor"]) - int(row["GoalsAgainst"]) for row in bottom_three
   132	    } == {-5}  # identical on the modern rule
   133	    assert [row["GoalAverage"] for row in bottom_three] == ["0.706", "0.667", "0.615"]
   134	
   135	
   136	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   137	# The alternative reading, also shipped: the table as it actually stood.
   138	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   139	
   140	
   141	def test_the_28_september_table_reflects_the_three_postponed_fixtures():
   142	    """Three round-9 games were postponed and played in Dec 1974 / Apr 1975.
   143	
   144	    So on the evening of matchday 10, six clubs had played only nine games. This
   145	    is the table as it truly stood, and the reason the submitted answer uses the
   146	    matchday reading instead. See README.md.
   147	    """
   148	    table = table_for(PLAYED_BY_28_SEP)
   149	    played_nine = {row["Team"] for row in table if row["Played"] == "9"}
   150	
   151	    assert played_nine == {
   152	        "Newcastle United",
   153	        "Middlesbrough",
   154	        "Leicester City",
   155	        "Leeds United",
   156	        "Arsenal",
   157	        "Tottenham Hotspur",
   158	    }
   159	    assert table[0]["Team"] == "Ipswich Town"  # top either way
   160	
   161	
   162	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   163	# The committed output files must match what the tool actually produces.
   164	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   165	
   166	
   167	def test_the_committed_output_files_are_what_the_tool_produces_today():
   168	    for results, expected in (
   169	        (ROUNDS_1_TO_10, STANDINGS_AFTER_MATCHDAY_10),
   170	        (PLAYED_BY_28_SEP, STANDINGS_AS_OF_28_SEP),
   171	    ):
   172	        produced = io.StringIO()
   173	
   174	        assert main([str(results)], stdout=produced) == EXIT_OK
   175	        assert produced.getvalue() == expected.read_text(), f"{expected.name} is stale"
   176	
   177	
   178	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   179	# The calendar reading, shipped so the ambiguity can be judged rather than
   180	# taken on trust -- and, as it happens, the strongest validation in the repo.
   181	# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
   182	
   183	PLAYED_BY_19_OCT = DATA / "results_1974-75_played_by_1974-10-19.csv"
   184	STANDINGS_AS_OF_19_OCT = DATA / "standings_1974-75_as_of_1974-10-19.csv"
   185	
   186	
   187	def test_the_tenth_calendar_week_table_matches_11v11s_published_table_exactly():
   188	    """Our table for 19 October 1974, against the one 11v11 publishes.
   189	
   190	    This is the closest thing to a ground truth available: a historical table,
   191	    published by an archive, that prints its own goal-average column and orders
   192	    the clubs by it. Every row below was read from
   193	    https://www.11v11.com/league-tables/league-division-one/19-october-1974/
   194	
   195	    It exercises everything at once -- the match data, the two-points rule, the
   196	    goal-average ratio, the ordering, and the handling of clubs on unequal games
   197	    played (six postponed fixtures leave Leicester on 12 and ten clubs on 13).
   198	
   199	    If any of that were wrong, this test could not pass.
   200	    """
   201	    published = [
   202	        # team, P, W, D, L, GF, GA, GAvg, Pts
   203	        ("Liverpool", "13", "9", "1", "3", "21", "8", "2.625", "19"),
   204	        ("Manchester City", "14", "8", "3", "3", "19", "15", "1.267", "19"),
   205	        ("Ipswich Town", "14", "8", "1", "5", "18", "9", "2.000", "17"),
   206	        ("Everton", "14", "4", "9", "1", "19", "16", "1.188", "17"),
   207	        ("Middlesbrough", "13", "6", "4", "3", "19", "14", "1.357", "16"),
   208	        ("Stoke City", "13", "6", "4", "3", "20", "15", "1.333", "16"),
   209	        ("Derby County", "14", "5", "6", "3", "21", "19", "1.105", "16"),
   210	        ("Burnley", "14", "7", "1", "6", "23", "23", "1.000", "15"),
   211	        ("Newcastle United", "13", "5", "5", "3", "19", "19", "1.000", "15"),
   212	        ("West Ham United", "14", "5", "4", "5", "25", "22", "1.136", "14"),
   213	        ("Birmingham City", "14", "6", "2", "6", "20", "20", "1.000", "14"),
   214	        ("Wolverhampton Wanderers", "14", "4", "6", "4", "15", "15", "1.000", "14"),
   215	        ("Sheffield United", "14", "5", "4", "5", "19", "24", "0.792", "14"),
   216	        ("Carlisle United", "14", "5", "3", "6", "12", "12", "1.000", "13"),
   217	        ("Coventry City", "13", "3", "6", "4", "18", "23", "0.783", "12"),
   218	        ("Leeds United", "13", "4", "3", "6", "16", "15", "1.067", "11"),
   219	        ("Chelsea", "13", "3", "5", "5", "13", "20", "0.650", "11"),
   220	        ("Leicester City", "12", "3", "4", "5", "16", "18", "0.889", "10"),
   221	        ("Tottenham Hotspur", "13", "4", "1", "8", "16", "20", "0.800", "9"),
   222	        ("Queens Park Rangers", "13", "2", "5", "6", "11", "16", "0.688", "9"),
   223	        ("Luton Town", "14", "1", "6", "7", "12", "21", "0.571", "8"),
   224	        ("Arsenal", "13", "2", "3", "8", "12", "20", "0.600", "7"),
   225	    ]
   226	
   227	    ours = [
   228	        (
   229	            row["Team"], row["Played"], row["Won"], row["Drawn"], row["Lost"],
   230	            row["GoalsFor"], row["GoalsAgainst"], row["GoalAverage"], row["Points"],
   231	        )
   232	        for row in table_for(PLAYED_BY_19_OCT)
   233	    ]
   234	
   235	    assert ours == published
   236	
   237	
   238	def test_the_postponements_leave_clubs_on_unequal_games_by_19_october():
   239	    # Two postponement clusters -- three round-9 fixtures and three round-13 --
   240	    # and Leicester City were caught by both.
   241	    table = table_for(PLAYED_BY_19_OCT)
   242	    by_games = {row["Team"]: row["Played"] for row in table}
   243	
   244	    assert by_games["Leicester City"] == "12"  # hit twice
   245	    assert sorted(by_games.values()).count("13") == 10
   246	    assert sorted(by_games.values()).count("14") == 11
   247	
   248	
   249	def test_the_committed_calendar_week_output_is_what_the_tool_produces_today():
   250	    produced = io.StringIO()
   251	
   252	    assert main([str(PLAYED_BY_19_OCT)], stdout=produced) == EXIT_OK

content.text: Confirmed the 11v11 table matches exactly. But look closely at that table: 11v11 prints **both** GAvg and GD, and I want to check whether its ordering actually discriminates between them. Let me mutate the sort to goal difference and see which tests catch it.

input.command:
set -e
S=/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-jramirezolmos-Documents-personal-span-league-table/ed232752-4a2f-47aa-9632-c8175fcab651/scratchpad
rm -rf $S/mut && cp -r /Users/jramirezolmos/Documents/personal/span-league-table $S/mut
cd $S/mut
python3 - <<'EOF'
import pathlib
p = pathlib.Path("src/league_table/table.py")
s = p.read_text()
s = s.replace("""    band, ratio = goal_average(tally.goals_for, tally.goals_against).sort_key()
    return (-tally.points, -band, -ratio, team)""",
"""    difference = tally.goals_for - tally.goals_against
    return (-tally.points, -difference, team)""")
